issue.title: workflows: use zip/unzip to keep execution permission
issue.body:
Use zip/unzip to keep execution permission when uploading and downloading artifacts.

The modification is not tested locally because I failed to setup github runner docker container through [act](https://github.com/nektos/act). The required space for the github runner is large.

Fixes #96.

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> Focuses on preserving execute permissions across jobs by zipping artifacts and unzipping where needed.
> 
> - `download_uast_binaries`: add `chmod +x`, zip `uast_binaries` and upload `uast-binaries.zip` instead of a folder
> - `resolve_uast_versions`: download `uast-binaries` artifact and `unzip` before deploying to `deps/`
> - `build_engine`: package `dist` + `node_modules` into `engine-<target>.zip` and upload the zip
> - `package_release`: download engine artifacts with `merge-multiple`, unzip all `engine-*.zip` and `uast-binaries.zip` before packaging platform bundles
> - Minor path/option adjustments to match zipped artifacts (no functional build changes)
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit b846f14ada51d751ced0c4cac207665edd4f80f0. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

comment.body:
> You can make a try using tar!

Loss of execution permission of uast* binaries are caused by YASA-UAST binary release I think. In [fzf](https://github.com/junegunn/fzf/releases/tag/v0.67.0), single binary is released in a zip/tar.gz to keep permission.

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -892,15 +892,15 @@ func (f *Fs) List(ctx context.Context, dir string) (entries fs.DirEntries, err e
 
 	resultchan := make(chan []*ftp.Entry, 1)
 	errchan := make(chan error, 1)
-	go func() {
+	go func(c *ftp.ServerConn) {
 		result, err := c.List(f.dirFromStandardPath(path.Join(f.root, dir)))
 		f.putFtpConnection(&c, err)
 		if err != nil {
 			errchan <- err
 			return
 		}
 		resultchan <- result
-	}()
+	}(c)
comment.body: A extreme solution might be adding `c = nil` or `drop(&c)` to move the ownership of `c` to goroutine. But the lifespan of `c` still depends on GC. So adding `c = nil` manually does not make much sense I think.

issue.title: small refactoring to reduce memory usage
issue.body:
<!--

Welcome :-)

So you've got an idea to improve rclone? We love that!
You'll be glad to hear we've incorporated hundreds of ideas from contributors already.

Probably the latest beta (or stable) release has your feature, so try to update your rclone.
The update instructions are available at https://rclone.org/commands/rclone_selfupdate/

If it still isn't there, here is a checklist of things to do:

  1. Search the old issues for your idea and +1 or comment on an existing issue if possible.
  2. Discuss on the forum: https://forum.rclone.org/
  3. Make a feature request issue (this is the right place!).
  4. Be prepared to get involved making the feature :-)

Looking forward to your great idea!

The Rclone Developers

-->

#### The associated forum post URL from `https://forum.rclone.org`

This change is small and easy to apply I think.

#### What is your current rclone version (output from `rclone version`)?

v1.72.1

#### What problem are you are trying to solve?

Removing unnecessary heap allocation.

#### How do you think rclone should be changed to solve that?

Just move the location to declare variables.

1. 3 variables in `WaitForStateContext`

https://github.com/rclone/rclone/blob/28c187b9b49a9785a7f47a802c74e8a8aedf11c6/backend/oracleobjectstorage/waiter.go#L63-L64
https://github.com/rclone/rclone/blob/28c187b9b49a9785a7f47a802c74e8a8aedf11c6/backend/oracleobjectstorage/waiter.go#L87

Currently, these variables will be allocated in heap, which consumes more memory. They are all used in `go func()` but not used in other places. So they can be declared in `go func()`.

2. change `=` to `:=`, which is a tiny change. 

https://github.com/rclone/rclone/blob/28c187b9b49a9785a7f47a802c74e8a8aedf11c6/cmd/serve/sftp/connection.go#L294

Because of `=`, the assignment op, `err` variable declared in `handleChannel` will be allocated in heap, which is unnecessary. Change `=` to `:=` to declare `err` in the function literal will avoid allocating it in heap. This is really a small change but it does work. 😂

<!--- Please keep the note below for others who read your feature request. -->

#### How to use GitHub

* Please use the 👍 [reaction](https://blog.github.com/2016-03-10-add-reactions-to-pull-requests-issues-and-comments/) to show that you are affected by the same issue.
* Please don't comment if you have no relevant information to add. It's just extra noise for everyone subscribed to this issue.
* Subscribe to receive notifications on status change and new comments.

comment.body:
The escape analysis log before changing is as follows. Only relevant lines are extracted here.

```
$ go build -a -gcflags=all=-m=2 . &> m2.log
$ rg -C 10 'backend/oracleobjectstorage/waiter.go:63' m2.log
backend/oracleobjectstorage/waiter.go:63:2: moved to heap: notfoundTick
backend/oracleobjectstorage/waiter.go:64:2: moved to heap: targetOccurrence
backend/oracleobjectstorage/waiter.go:87:2: moved to heap: result
$ rg 'cmd/serve/sftp/connection.go:254' m2.log
cmd/serve/sftp/connection.go:254:21: moved to heap: err
```

After changing, `notfoundTick`, `targetOccurrence`, `result`, `err` are allocated in stack.

```
$ go build -a -gcflags=all=-m=2 . &> m2.log
$ rg 'notfoundTick' m2.log # No output with return code 1
$ rg 'targetOccurrence' m2.log # No output with return code 1
$ rg 'backend/oracleobjectstorage/waiter.go:87' m2.log # No output with return code 1. In PR#9079, the line of new definition of `result` is 87. see https://github.com/rclone/rclone/pull/9079/changes#diff-d09350928b2530376c41a5714eedf5d9e2a2922fe250194f317900e3e07530a3R87
$ rg 'cmd/serve/sftp/connection.go:254' m2.log # No output again. In PR#9079, the line of `err` definition does not change. You can see https://github.com/rclone/rclone/pull/9079/changes#diff-853a5af09efd80d0bfa2aa41c0ad00a48c3937258a3676780fc56e192c899cd9L254
```

I think the log proves the tiny improvement.

---

There is another variable whose type is pointer is allocated in heap and can be allocated in stack through code changing. But I don't change that in PR.

https://github.com/rclone/rclone/blob/28c187b9b49a9785a7f47a802c74e8a8aedf11c6/backend/ftp/ftp.go#L883-L903

```
$ rg 'backend/ftp/ftp.go:885' m2.log
1578890:backend/ftp/ftp.go:885:2: (*Fs).List capturing by ref: c (addr=true assign=false width=8)
1578891:backend/ftp/ftp.go:885:2: c escapes to heap:
1578892:backend/ftp/ftp.go:885:2:   flow: {storage for func literal} = &c:
1578893:backend/ftp/ftp.go:885:2:     from c (captured by a closure) at backend/ftp/ftp.go:896:18
1578894:backend/ftp/ftp.go:885:2:     from c (reference) at backend/ftp/ftp.go:896:18
1578952:backend/ftp/ftp.go:885:2: moved to heap: c
```

In the above code, `c`, which is moved to heap, can be passed to the goroutine through argument to avoid the pointer object `c` itself allocated in heap. To avoid `c` still references underlying object, the code can be changed as follows just like what `*pc = nil` in `putFtpConnection(&c, err)` does.

```go
	go func(c *ftp.ServerCon) {
		result, err := c.List(f.dirFromStandardPath(path.Join(f.root, dir)))
		f.putFtpConnection(&c, err)
		if err != nil {
			errchan <- err
			return
		}
		resultchan <- result
	}(c)
    c = nil
```

~~I should have shown the assembly where heap allocation function `newobject` can be found, but the assembly is too large.~~ I made it. Details are shown as follows.

<details>
    <summary>assembly of "ftp.(*Fs).List" of code before change. c is allocated in heap</summary>

```bash
$ go tool objdump -S rclone > rclone.plan9
$ rg -A 40 'rclone/backend/ftp.\(\*Fs\).List\(SB\)' rclone.plan9 # only relevant line are extracted here. comments are added.
8569382:TEXT github.com/rclone/rclone/backend/ftp.(*Fs).List(SB) /home/lqw/mygit/QLStat/repos/github.com/rclone/backend/ftp/ftp.go
8569383-func (f *Fs) List(ctx context.Context, dir string) (entries fs.DirEntries, err error) {
8569384-  0x2385f80             4c8da42400ffffff        LEAQ 0xffffff00(SP), R12
8569385-  0x2385f88             4d3b6610                CMPQ R12, 0x10(R14)
8569386-  0x2385f8c             0f861d090000            JBE 0x23868af
8569387-  0x2385f92             55                      PUSHQ BP
8569388-  0x2385f93             4889e5                  MOVQ SP, BP
8569389-  0x2385f96             4881ec78010000          SUBQ $0x178, SP
8569390-        if err != nil {
8569391-  0x2385f9d             4889842488010000        MOVQ AX, 0x188(SP)
8569392-  0x2385fa5             4889b424a8010000        MOVQ SI, 0x1a8(SP)
8569393-  0x2385fad             4889bc24a0010000        MOVQ DI, 0x1a0(SP)
8569394-  0x2385fb5             48898c2498010000        MOVQ CX, 0x198(SP)
8569395-  0x2385fbd             48899c2490010000        MOVQ BX, 0x190(SP)
8569396-        c, err := f.getFtpConnection(ctx)
8569397-  0x2385fc5             488d0534a1b901          LEAQ type:ymgbKdzc(SB), AX
8569398-  0x2385fcc             e8cfcb08ff              CALL runtime.newobject(SB)      # `c` pointer object is allocated in heap.
8569399-  0x2385fd1             4889842470010000        MOVQ AX, 0x170(SP)               # `&c` is stored in 0x170(SP)
8569400-  0x2385fd9             440f11bc2450010000      MOVUPS X15, 0x150(SP)
8569401-  0x2385fe2             488b842488010000        MOVQ 0x188(SP), AX
8569402-  0x2385fea             488b9c2490010000        MOVQ 0x190(SP), BX
8569403-  0x2385ff2             488b8c2498010000        MOVQ 0x198(SP), CX
8569404-  0x2385ffa             e861ddffff              CALL github.com/rclone/rclone/backend/ftp.(*Fs).getFtpConnection(SB)   # return value of getFtpConnection is stored in AX
8569405-  0x2385fff             48899c2450010000        MOVQ BX, 0x150(SP)
8569406-  0x2386007             48898c2458010000        MOVQ CX, 0x158(SP)
8569407-  0x238600f             833dfa28c00300          CMPL runtime.writeBarrier(SB), $0x0
8569408-  0x2386016             750a                    JNE 0x2386022
8569409-  0x2386018             488b942470010000        MOVQ 0x170(SP), DX              # `&c` moved to DX
8569410-  0x2386020             eb17                    JMP 0x2386039
8569411-  0x2386022             e839960fff              CALL local.runtime.gcWriteBarrier2(SB)
8569412-  0x2386027             498903                  MOVQ AX, 0(R11)
8569413-  0x238602a             488b942470010000        MOVQ 0x170(SP), DX
8569414-  0x2386032             4c8b02                  MOVQ 0(DX), R8
8569415-  0x2386035             4d894308                MOVQ R8, 0x8(R11)
8569416-  0x2386039             488902                  MOVQ AX, 0(DX)                    # return value of getFtpConnection is stored in `c` which is allocated in heap.
```
</details>

<details>
    <summary>assembly of "ftp.(*Fs).List" of code after change. c is allocated in stack.</summary>

```bash
$ go build; go tool objdump -S rclone > rclone-opt.plan9
$ rg -A 50 'rclone/backend/ftp.\(\*Fs\).List\(SB\)' rclone-opt.plan9
8569422-        c, err := f.getFtpConnection(ctx)
8569423-  0x238604e             48898424d8000000        MOVQ AX, 0xd8(SP) # AX is return value of getFtpConnection and is stored into stack
```

</details>

Pardon the shameless plug, but I've developed a small linter that handles this issue. I come from the static analysis tool [CodeQL](https://codeql.github.com/) with [escape analysis extension](https://github.com/Lslightly/QLStat/blob/77dd3a4b2068e3f0346346c0d48a511f5342a21c/cmd/escape_adapter/doc.go) which combines the escape analysis log and CodeQL database. Based on the [extension](https://github.com/Lslightly/QLStat/blob/77dd3a4b2068e3f0346346c0d48a511f5342a21c/cmd/escape_adapter/doc.go) and the [heapvar_should_move.ql](https://github.com/Lslightly/QLStat/blob/main/qlsrc/escape_ext/heapvar_should_move.ql), I find these opportunities of optimization. For rclone, there are only 5 locations which seems not hot code.

forkee.name: YASA-Engine
forkee.description: YASA is an open-source static program analysis project. Its core innovation lies in a unified intermediate representation called UAST, designed to support multiple programming languages. Built on top of UAST, YASA provides a highly accurate static analysis framework.

issue.title: binaries in yasa-linux-x64.zip have no execution permission
issue.body:
I downloaded [yasa-linux-x64.zip](https://github.com/antgroup/YASA-Engine/releases/download/v0.2.30/yasa-linux-x64.zip) but found that binaries did not have execution permission.

```bash
$ zipinfo yasa-linux-x64.zip
-rw-r--r--  3.0 unx  4324099 b- defN 25-Nov-28 05:25 uast4go-linux-amd64
-rw-r--r--  3.0 unx 24817408 b- defN 25-Nov-28 05:25 uast4py-linux-amd64
-rw-r--r--  3.0 unx 273963615 b- defN 25-Nov-28 05:25 yasa-engine-linux-x64
```

Execution permission might get loss after [uploading artifact](https://github.com/antgroup/YASA-Engine/blob/44269b02dd77869008df2e13b26ddbf984d2454c/.github/workflows/release.yml#L353-L360) as [Permission Loss](https://github.com/actions/upload-artifact?tab=readme-ov-file#permission-loss) mentions if I understand correctly.

issue.title: telemetry report: failed allocation in loadArrayValues
issue.body:
```
#!stacks
"runtime.mallocgc" && "loadArrayValues" && "EvalVariableInScope"
```
Issue created by [stacks](https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/telemetry/cmd/stacks).

This stack `qXHBFw` was [reported by telemetry](https://telemetry.go.dev/data/2026-01-15):

- `crash/crash`
- `runtime.systemstack_switch:+4`
- [`runtime.(*mheap).alloc:+5`](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/go1.25.4:src/runtime/mheap.go;l=1011)
- [`runtime.(*mcache).allocLarge:+15`](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/go1.25.4:src/runtime/mcache.go;l=236)
- [`runtime.mallocgcLarge:+16`](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/go1.25.4:src/runtime/malloc.go;l=1552)
- [`runtime.mallocgc:+58`](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/go1.25.4:src/runtime/malloc.go;l=1072)
- [`runtime.growslice:+95`](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/go1.25.4:src/runtime/slice.go;l=272)
- [`github.com/go-delve/delve/pkg/proc.(*Variable).loadArrayValues:+42`](https://github.com/go-delve/delve/blob/v1.26.0/pkg/proc/variables.go#L1759)
- [`github.com/go-delve/delve/pkg/proc.(*Variable).loadValueInternal:+78`](https://github.com/go-delve/delve/blob/v1.26.0/pkg/proc/variables.go#L1416)

- [`github.com/go-delve/delve/pkg/proc.(*evalStack).result:+13`](https://github.com/go-delve/delve/blob/v1.26.0/pkg/proc/eval.go#L1090)
- [`github.com/go-delve/delve/pkg/proc.(*EvalScope).EvalExpression:+10`](https://github.com/go-delve/delve/blob/v1.26.0/pkg/proc/eval.go#L210)

issue.title: VSCode + Go 1.25.5 + dlv 1.26.0 debug don't work
issue.body:
### What version of Delve are you using (`dlv version`)?

1.26.0 Build: $Id: 7fd7302eab8b16d715a94af1b5dfbffc2e1359bc $

### What version of Go are you using? (`go version`)?

go1.25.5 windows/amd64

### What operating system and processor architecture are you using?

Windows 11 + AMD Ryzen 9 7945HX3D with Radeon Graphics

### Description

VSCode Version

1.108.1 (Commit: 585eba7c0c34fd6b30faac7c62a42050bfbc0086, x64)

If this is about source listing not showing up (or breakpoints not being accepted) while running in a container please read our FAQ first.

1. **What did you do?**

go.mod

```
module hello

go 1.25

```

hello.go

```Go
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"time"
)

func main() {
	fmt.Println("before")
	time.Sleep(1 * time.Second)
	fmt.Println("after")
}

```

   VSCODE + F5 -> DEBUG


2. **What did you expect to see?**
   
   Expected to be able to debug with breakpoints.
   
3. **What did you see instead?**

   The program skipped directly, breakpoints were not triggered.

```
Starting: C:\Users\cShar\go\bin\dlv.exe dap --listen=127.0.0.1:57959 from e:\Study\hello
DAP server listening at: 127.0.0.1:57959
Type 'dlv help' for list of commands.
before
after
Process 50048 has exited with status 0
Detaching
```

comment.body: Another thing, could you run `go build -gcflags='all=-N -l'` in the `e:\Study\hello` directory and upload the resulting binary file somewhere?

issue.title: Option to show nested variable binding types without full module path?
issue.body: Hi there, and thanks for providing this tool! Using Delve DAP via VS Code, I'm working on some deeply nested data structures. While the first level of bindings have their type information displayed as `package.Type`, any level of nesting below shows the full module path prepended. This makes debugging this code quite annoying as I constantly have to widen the view and click to expand items that would otherwise have been visible directly. Is there a setting somewhere to use the `package.Type` format for display of bindings at all levels of nesting? Or is this a feature that could be considered?

issue.title: Option to show nested variable binding types without full module path?

Is there a setting somewhere to use the `package.Type` format for display of bindings at all levels of nesting? Or is this a feature that could be considered?

issue.title: VSCode + Go 1.25.5 + dlv 1.26.0 debug don't work
issue.body:
### What version of Delve are you using (`dlv version`)?

1.26.0 Build: $Id: 7fd7302eab8b16d715a94af1b5dfbffc2e1359bc $

### What version of Go are you using? (`go version`)?

go1.25.5 windows/amd64

### What operating system and processor architecture are you using?

Windows 11 + AMD Ryzen 9 7945HX3D with Radeon Graphics

### Description

VSCode Version

1.108.1 (Commit: 585eba7c0c34fd6b30faac7c62a42050bfbc0086, x64)

If this is about source listing not showing up (or breakpoints not being accepted) while running in a container please read our FAQ first.

1. **What did you do?**

go.mod

```
module hello

go 1.25

```

hello.go

```Go
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"time"
)

func main() {
	fmt.Println("before")
	time.Sleep(1 * time.Second)
	fmt.Println("after")
}

```

   VSCODE + F5 -> DEBUG


2. **What did you expect to see?**
   
   Expected to be able to debug with breakpoints.
   
3. **What did you see instead?**

   The program skipped directly, breakpoints were not triggered.

```
Starting: C:\Users\cShar\go\bin\dlv.exe dap --listen=127.0.0.1:57959 from e:\Study\hello
DAP server listening at: 127.0.0.1:57959
Type 'dlv help' for list of commands.
before
after
Process 50048 has exited with status 0
Detaching
```

comment.body: If you run `dlv substitute-path-guess-helper` from the directory `e:\Study\hello` what's the output?
The change to ProducerAfterOrEqual looks good to me.

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -357,7 +357,7 @@ func (v *Variable) writeMapTo(buf io.Writer, flags prettyFlags, indent, fmtstr s
 
 		key.writeTo(buf, 0, indent+indentString, fmtstr)
 		fmt.Fprint(buf, ": ")
-		value.writeTo(buf, prettyFlags(0).set(prettyNewlines, nl), indent+indentString, fmtstr)
+		value.writeTo(buf, prettyFlags(0).set(prettyShortenType, flags.shortenType()).set(prettyNewlines, nl), indent+indentString, fmtstr)
comment.body: Doesn't this mean that types will always be printed shortened, no matter what the original flags said?
This makes no difference, the version of the test harness and the version of the executable is always the same. We use `runtime.Version` everywhere for tests.

issue.title: VSCode: Failed to launch: could not launch process: not an executable file
issue.body: ### What version of Delve are you using (`dlv version`)? 1.25.2; build af3df277866d7175e816eab14e56611053d4cdea ### What version of Go are you using? (`go version`)? go version go1.25.3 X:nodwarf5 linux/amd64 ### What operating system and processor architecture are you using? Gentoo Linux 6.12.41, Intel x86_64, Wayland, KDE ### Description Sometimes when I attempt to launch my executable via VSCode I get this error message: Debug console: > Starting: $GOBIN/dlv dap --listen=127.0.0.1:42255 --log-dest=3 from /my/executable > DAP server listening at: 127.0.0.1:42255 In this particular case, my debug configuration is: ```json { "name": "Run", "type": "go", "request": "launch", "mode": "auto", "program": "${workspaceFolder}" } ``` But I've had this happen for many different configurations and I haven't been able to identify a parameter that triggers this. Part of the issue is that it happens more or less randomly; that is, I can't intentionally reproduce it. It just happens, whenever the gremlins in my computer feel like it. **If I change `launch.json` *at all* I'm able to launch again.** Just now, I added then deleted white space (outside of any property or string), saved the file, and the error stopped happening. So there's definitely something weird happening, potentially related to vscode-go. I'm assuming this error report is not enough information to do anything about it; I guess I'm really asking for help/advice on how to debug this. I tried launching delve with delve, but that did not reproduce the issue. Also I haven't been able to reproduce the issue by directly launching with delve. Certainly the issue could be related to vscode-go, but I imagine it could also be some subtle caching issue, or something related to DAP mode, or all of the above. I've tried launching separately DAP mode then having vscode-go attach to that existing session, but I wasn't able to get that to work and I'm not sure it would reproduce the issue. Should I create a custom build/install of delve that includes debugging symbols and is unoptimized? Is it possible to tell delve to pause until a debugger attaches to it?

issue.body: ### What version of Delve are you using (`dlv version`)? 1.25.2; build af3df277866d7175e816eab14e56611053d4cdea ### What version of Go are you using? (`go version`)? go version go1.25.3 X:nodwarf5 linux/amd64 ### What operating system and processor architecture are you using? Gentoo Linux 6.12.41, Intel x86_64, Wayland, KDE ### Description Sometimes when I attempt to launch my executable via VSCode I get this error message: Debug console: > Starting: $GOBIN/dlv dap --listen=127.0.0.1:42255 --log-dest=3 from /my/executable > DAP server listening at: 127.0.0.1:42255 In this particular case, my debug configuration is: ```json { "name": "Run", "type": "go", "request": "launch", "mode": "auto", "program": "${workspaceFolder}" } ``` But I've had this happen for many different configurations and I haven't been able to identify a parameter that triggers this. Part of the issue is that it happens more or less randomly; that is, I can't intentionally reproduce it. It just happens, whenever the gremlins in my computer feel like it. **If I change `launch.json` *at all* I'm able to launch again.** Just now, I added then deleted white space (outside of any property or string), saved the file, and the error stopped happening. So there's definitely something weird happening, potentially related to vscode-go. I'm assuming this error report is not enough information to do anything about it; I guess I'm really asking for help/advice on how to debug this. I tried launching delve with delve, but that did not reproduce the issue. Also I haven't been able to reproduce the issue by directly launching with delve. Certainly the issue could be related to vscode-go, but I imagine it could also be some subtle caching issue, or something related to DAP mode, or all of the above. I've tried launching separately DAP mode then having vscode-go attach to that existing session, but I wasn't able to get that to work and I'm not sure it would reproduce the issue. Should I create a custom build/install of delve that includes debugging symbols and is unoptimized? Is it possible to tell delve to pause until a debugger attaches to it?

comment.body: That error should come from the `verifyBinaryFormat` function in `service/debugger/debugger.go`. It just tries to open the file as an elf file (in your case). It sounds like the compiler produced a malformed executable, altough I don't know how it can be possible. You could change the function `Remove` in `pkg/gobuild/gobuild.go` to do nothing and when the bug happens examine the binary file that delve produced manually.

review.body: LGTM

issue.title: [BUG] BigInt parses “-0” as negative zero in non‑JS backend

labels.name: bug
labels.description: Something isn't working

issue.body:
In the non‑JS implementation, BigInt::from_string("-0") and BigInt::from_hex("-0") produce a "negative zero".to_string() returns "0", but the internal sign remains negative, so equality and comparison against 0N are inconsistent.

### Impact
- @bigint.BigInt::from_string("-0") == 0N is false
- @bigint.BigInt::from_hex("-0") == 0N is false
- compare(0N) returns -1
- to_string() returns "0"
- JS backend doesn’t have this issue (`-0n == 0n` is true)

```mbt
test "from_string negative zero" {
  let z = @bigint.BigInt::from_string("-0")
  inspect(z.to_string(), content="0")
  assert_true(z == 0N)
  assert_eq(z.compare(0N), 0)
}
test "from_hex negative zero" {
  let z = @bigint.BigInt::from_hex("-0")
  inspect(z.to_string(), content="0")
  assert_true(z == 0N)
  assert_eq(z.compare(0N), 0)
}
```

issue.title: [BUG] Deque::retain / Deque::retain_map do not clear when full and wrapped

labels.name: bug
labels.description: Something isn't working

issue.body:
When a deque is full and wrapped (head != 0), calling retain(_ => false) or retain_map(_ => None) does not clear it. The length stays non‑zero and views still contain the original elements.

```moonbit
test "retain full wrapped" {
  let dq = @deque.new(capacity=4)
  dq..push_back(1)..push_back(2)..push_back(3)..push_back(4)
  ignore(dq.pop_front())
  dq.push_back(5) // full + wrapped
  dq.retain(_ => false)
  inspect(dq.as_views(), content="([], [])")
}

test "retain_map full wrapped" {
  let dq = @deque.new(capacity=4)
  dq..push_back(1)..push_back(2)..push_back(3)..push_back(4)
  ignore(dq.pop_front())
  dq.push_back(5) // full + wrapped
  dq.retain_map(_ => None)
  inspect(dq.as_views(), content="([], [])")
}
```

Expected

  - retain(false) / retain_map(None) clears the deque
  - dq.is_empty() is true
  - dq.as_views() returns ([], [])

  Actual

  - dq.as_views() stays ([2, 3, 4], [5])
  - dq.is_empty() is false

issue.title: Add clarifying text to SKILL.md
issue.body:
I have been using different LLM agents to write MoonBit code, and have discovered that they frequently make the same mistakes over and over again when starting new sessions, so this repo is of keen interest to me.

I've attempted to add some clarifying text to the "SKILL.md" file in the hopes of reducing frequently-made mistakes by LLM agents. Several changes were actually made by "moon fmt" and not by me.

I'm happy to iterate on these changes if there are any that you don't agree with.

comment.body:
> Also, @bobzhang - shouldn't "moon help" be mentioned somewhere? I can't seem to find it anywhere.

this is not supposed to be manual, we may remove some of them when newer models have more built in knowledge about MoonBit

summary_topics.work: The user's primary technical interest centers on formal methods and program verification, specifically loop invariants. The repository suggests engagement with MoonBit, a language focused on correctness and safety, indicating contributions to foundational aspects of verified software development. Core domains include programming language theory, static analysis, and ensuring code reliability through mathematical rigor.

issue.body:
First noticed on some Gerrit changes in presubmit.

Example failures:
- https://ci.chromium.org/ui/p/golang/builders/try/gotip-linux-386/b8692676113130842481/overview
- https://ci.chromium.org/ui/p/golang/builders/try/gotip-linux-amd64/b8692675920058132961/overview
- https://ci.chromium.org/ui/p/golang/builders/try/gotip-linux-arm64/b8692676420959567137/overview

`cas` output looks like:
```
goroutine 1:
#0 go.chromium.org/luci/client/cmd/cas/casimpl/download.go:515 - casimpl.(*downloadRun).doDownload()
  reason: failed to download files

#1 go.chromium.org/luci/client/cmd/cas/casimpl/download.go:574 - casimpl.(*downloadRun).Run()
#2 github.com/maruel/subcommands@v1.1.1/subcommands.go:395 - subcommands.Run()
#3 cas/main.go:95 - main.main()
#4 runtime/proc.go:271 - runtime.main()
#5 runtime/asm_arm64.s:1222 - runtime.goexit()
```

We're currently trying to figure out the problem and mitigate the issue.

issue.title: x/build: perf.go.dev/dashboard fails to report x/tools benchmarks

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: gopls
labels.description: Issues related to the Go language server, gopls.

issue.body: The https://perf.golang.org/dashboard/?repository=tools page usually shows the benchmarks for x/tools including those of gopls, but it is currently failing to find any benchmark data:

comment.body:
Failures prevent uploads of dashboard data because we're worried about hard-to-identify bad data slipping into the database from broken code. A failure is at least _some_ signal that something may be off. (Not _especially_ worried, but I believe this was the original reasoning, and it was only marginally preferred.) I'm still a little hesitant to do this today _in general_ since it's especially bad for computing geomeans. Though, I don't think we compute any geomeans for x/tools, so perhaps taking the data is just fine for x/tools.

FTR, I do think we should either remove, skip, or fix the remaining failures. If a benchmark is broken and nobody's going to fix it, I don't see the point of keeping it around. (Does it succeed locally? Is it a builder issue?)

issue.title: x/build: add a builder for GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
@mknyszek suggested it likely could be worthwhile to add a builder that enables `GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc` in order to help with coverage.

`GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc` is a disabled-by-default `GOEXPERIMENT` in Go 1.26. It currently includes changes in the runtime and compiler. 

(For an overview, see the "Introduction" section in https://go.dev/design/74299-runtime-freegc, or see https://go.dev/cl/710015 for some sample performance results.)

Filing this issue for discussion and tracking.

Thanks!

CC @dmitshur, @golang/release

comment.body:
I mean, I think running this in presubmit would be worthwhile to catch breakage and conflicts in features early, I just don't have a good sense of which parts of the compiler this matters for, haha. We can always revisit it if it gets annoying or there's frequent breakage, it's not hard to add.

Yeah, `watchflakes` reports ~every failure (as long as it's not broken...) so it'll all end up on GitHub.

Given that this touches both the runtime and compiler side, I didn't have it run anywhere in particular on presubmit by default since it's not obvious exactly where I should have it run. If it becomes a problem (builder breaks, though I expect not) we can always add that later. In the meantime, please follow the https://go.dev/wiki/SlowBots guide for ensuring the builder gets run with your changes. (It will get run in postsubmit, visible on the dashboard at https://build.golang.org, as usual.)

issue.title: cmd/go: go build -json

labels.name: GoCommand
labels.description: cmd/go

issue.body:
(Edited 2024-01-18: Changed `PackageID` to `ImportPath`.)

I propose we add structured JSON output to `go build`, similar to and compatible with the structured JSON output of `go test -json`. This JSON output will be enabled by a new `go build -json` flag, and also implicitly enabled for all builds done on behalf of `go test -json`. This proposal aims to address #23037 and #35169, as well as improve structured all.bash output (#37486).

The main motivation for this proposal is to enable build output that is consistent and compatible with `go test -json` output. Currently, if a test or imported package fails to build during `go test -json`, the build error text will be interleaved with the JSON output of tests. Furthermore, there’s currently no way to reliably associate a build error with the test package or packages it affected. This creates unnecessary friction and complexity in tools that consume the `go test -json` output.

For reference, the `go test -json` format is as follow:

```go
type TestEvent struct {
	Time    time.Time // encodes as an RFC3339-format string
	Action  string
	Package string
	Test    string
	Elapsed float64 // seconds
	Output  string
}
```

A detailed description can be found in [`go doc test2json`](https://pkg.go.dev/cmd/test2json).

## Proposal

There are two related parts to this proposal.

1. I propose we add a `-json` flag to `go build` that suppresses the text build errors written to stderr and instead writes a JSON-encoded stream to stdout described by the following type:

```go
type BuildEvent struct {
	ImportPath string
	Action     string
	Output     string `json:",omitempty"` // Non-empty if Action == “build-output”
}
```

The `ImportPath` field gives the package ID of the package being built. This matches the somewhat misnamed [`Package.ImportPath`](https://pkg.go.dev/go/build#Package) field of `go list -json` and what [golang.org/x/tools/go/packages](https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/tools/go/packages) calls the “ID”. This differs from the `TestEvent.Package` field, which is a plain import path, but using the full package ID is important for disambiguating errors from different builds and for consistency with the rest of the build process.

The `Action` field is one of the following:

- build-output - The toolchain printed output
- build-fail - The build failed

The `Output` field is set for `Action == "build-output"` and is defined exactly the same way as the `TestEvent.Output` field. A single event may contain one or more lines of output and there may be more than one output event for a given package ID.

This struct is designed so that parsers can distinguish interleaved TestEvents and BuildEvents by inspecting the `Action` field. Furthermore, as with TestEvent, parsers can simply concatenate the `Output` fields of all events to reconstruct the text format output, as it would have appeared from `go build` without the `-json` flag. (As a consequence, this means the output includes all post-processing done by the go tool, including path rewriting and adding the “# package” header before each package.)

Environmental errors (e.g., bad GOOS values, file system errors) and module-level errors (e.g., syntax errors in go.mod) will still be printed in plain text to stderr by the go command. These errors cause an immediate exit and happen very early, so they should never appear with JSON output.

2. I propose we add an optional `FailedBuild` field to `TestEvent` to connect tests that fail because of build errors back to the build error. When a test fails due to a build error, the go command would emit the usual "start" event for the test package, then an "output" event giving the "FAIL _package name_ [build failed]" or equivalent message, followed by a "fail" event with the new `FailedBuild` field set to the package ID of the package that failed to build.

This same approach can be used for setup failures as well, which print a "FAIL _package name_ [setup failed]" message. Setup failures reflect errors that prevent the go command from even invoking the compiler, such as errors in imports, but they are still logically build failures.

This approach to reporting build failures that affect tests tries to balance several considerations:
1. A test may fail because of a build failure in an imported package. Thus, we can’t assume that the build failure is in the test package itself.
1. A view that narrows down a large test log to "why did this particular test fail?" (a common feature of CI systems) should be able to report the text of the build failure, even if it wasn't in the test package itself. Thus, we need some way to connect each failing test package back to the root build failure.
1. Multiple test packages may fail because of a build failure in one common imported package. Preferably, the details of the build failure would appear only once in the log, regardless of how many tests fail because of it.
1. Consumers must be able to concatenate the Output fields to recover the original text output. Hence, the current "FAIL" line has to appear in some output event. This also forces us to report the details of each build failure only once, since that’s how it appears in the text output.
1. CI systems should be able to correlate the same test package across test logs from different commits, even if the test package runs in some logs and doesn’t run because of build failures in others. Since TestEvents use package paths and BuildEvents use package IDs, that means the CI system needs some way to get the package path of the failing test. Preferably this can be done without parsing the package ID.
1. Sequential test order should still be recoverable from the log with build failures. Hence, the "start" TestEvents should still appear and be in order.


## Example

Given [a test package](https://go.dev/play/p/wbqPTeaSljd) with a simple build error, currently `go test -json` emits the following:

```
# github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror [github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror.test]
./main_test.go:6:2: undefined: x
FAIL	github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror [build failed]
```

This is, in fact, identical to the output without the `-json` flag.

With the proposal, `go test -json` would instead print:

```
{"ImportPath":"github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror [github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror.test]","Action":"build-output","Output":"./main_test.go:6:2: undefined: x\n"}
{"ImportPath":"github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror [github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror.test]","Action":"build-fail"}
{"Time":"...","Action":"start","Package":"github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror"}
{"Time":"...","Action":"output","Package":"github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror","Output":"FAIL\tgithub.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror [build failed]\n"}
{"Time":"...","Action":"fail","Package":"github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror","FailedBuild":"github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror [github.com/aclements/gotest/testdata/builderror.test]"}
```

## Open issues and questions

Should we emit BuildEvents for successful package builds, like we emit TestEvents for passing tests? Those could include useful information like timing. If we think of this as akin to verbose output, do we emit start events, too? I did not include these in the proposal because they aren’t necessary to solve the immediate problems of processing `go test -json` output, but we could add these events in the future.

We could emit environmental and module-level errors in JSON. Above I proposed that these continue to be printed in text to stderr because they are fatal and happen before any build errors that would be printed in JSON, making them easy for tooling to process as text. From an implementation standpoint, there are also simply a huge number of possible causes for early, fatal errors, not all of which are even under the control of the go command, making it difficult to capture all of them. If we wanted to capture more errors, we would have to complicate `BuildEvent` to specify their sources, probably by making the `Package` field optional and adding an optional `Module` field. Module-level errors would populate the `Module` field and environmental errors would omit both fields. This all seems like unnecessary complexity.

`BuildEvent.ImportPath` and `TestEvent.Package` are defined slightly differently. There are good reasons for this (given above), and I chose different field names to help make this clear, but it may be confusing for consumers.

Finally, we could make the JSON build output much *more* structured, for example by encoding path and line information of errors as JSON fields. We chose to simply wrap the text output of the compiler because of the degree of variation in compiler output, including sub-errors and optional debug output.

comment.body:
@cherrymui pointed out to me that `GOCACHEPROG` (which I completely forgot existed) might make it so that we can try and remove some of the extra rebuild costs from sharding. It might take some work to set up, but it's an interesting possibility.

(Still, observability into build times remains useful.)

comment.body:
For our own test infrastructure, the lack of build timings is turning out to be a bit problematic. Just building tests and their dependencies may account for a significant share of test time, and there's a hidden cost to sharding tests: more dependencies need to be rebuilt (the total work of the system increases).

Unfortunately that cost is truly hidden today. In fact, my point about build caching is just a guess right now. Since we don't emit this information, we also don't collect it, so there's no way (yet) to know that this is the cause without adding my own instrumentation, and splitting the build step from the run step in `go test` and `go tool dist test`.

Note that the build timings are still useful in the presence of sharding because we always test the same packages together (under the same weighting scheme), so the build cache state is likely to be identical at the point of testing each package run to run.

issue.title: x/build: add -longcmdgo TryBot

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: NeedsDecision
labels.description: Feedback is required from experts, contributors, and/or the community before a change can be made.

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
(This issue is inspired by #76768: it's basically longcrypto but for the go command)

We run longtests for CLs affecting files in the cmd/go tree, because many of the cmd/go tests are not run in short mode (for instance because they run builds). But running all the longtests is really slow.

I'd like for us to add a longcmdgo TryBot that runs cmd/go/... tests without -short, but runs the rest of the tests in short mode.

cc @golang/release

comment.body: I sent a CL to double the longtest shard count and try to mitigate the "it's slow" part of the problem.

issue.title: x/build: add -longcrypto TryBot

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: NeedsDecision
labels.description: Feedback is required from experts, contributors, and/or the community before a change can be made.

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
Currently, the -longtest TryBots run on CLs that touch crypto/tls, to run the slow-ish BoGo test suite.

Also, cmd/dist runs the `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...` tests only in long mode.

This is sub-optimal in two ways: CLs that touch crypto/tls are excruciatingly slow to TryBot, and flake and suffer from broken builders all the time; meanwhile, most crypto CLs are not tested against older versions of the FIPS module.

Can we add a new TryBot which is enabled for all CLs that touch crypto/... and that tests in long mode only crypto/... and `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...`?

/cc @golang/release @golang/security

comment.body: We're working on the Commit-Queue+2 story to mitigate issues with persistent failures, but for now, a fairly easy improvement is just bumping the longtest shard count to try and mitigate the "excruciatingly slow to TryBot" part of the issue and reduce pain in the short term. I don't see a reason not to do it.

issue.title: x/build: add -longcrypto TryBot

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: NeedsDecision
labels.description: Feedback is required from experts, contributors, and/or the community before a change can be made.

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
Currently, the -longtest TryBots run on CLs that touch crypto/tls, to run the slow-ish BoGo test suite.

Also, cmd/dist runs the `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...` tests only in long mode.

This is sub-optimal in two ways: CLs that touch crypto/tls are excruciatingly slow to TryBot, and flake and suffer from broken builders all the time; meanwhile, most crypto CLs are not tested against older versions of the FIPS module.

Can we add a new TryBot which is enabled for all CLs that touch crypto/... and that tests in long mode only crypto/... and `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...`?

/cc @golang/release @golang/security

comment.body:
> As go.dev/s/needs-review is implemented today, we don't get Googler +1s until we have TryBot-Result+1 and Code-Review+2, so that just doesn't work today. Assuming we change needs-review to listing Code-Review+2 CLs without TryBot-Result+1, I am still very unlikely to follow this workflow, because it's a bet that I didn't break anything that TryBots would catch: if I win, I avoid a TryBot retry cycle, if I lose, I pay a _multi-day_ Googler +1 cycle. Totally not worth it.

When I say small changes, I really do mean like documentation updates to untested documentation (then again I did say "tested locally" -- my bad). But fair enough, how this integrates with go.dev/s/needs-review still needs figuring out. (And yes, it will likely involve just requiring passing Commit-Queue+1 anyway.)

> (The elephant in the room is that the Googler +1s are a major drag on non-Google maintainers which create meaningfully different perceptions of the cost of certain workflows, but I remember enough about how they came to be not to go down that path.)

For sure. Not much we can do about that ourselves right now.

> How is that no different? There are two interrupt sessions instead of one, one to restart Commit-Queue+1 until it passes, and one to restart Commit-Queue+2 until it passes. Again assuming true random flakes, these are uncorrelated and so their probabilities just combine, causing double the pain (even if we assume longtest has the same flakiness as TryBot, which I suspect it does not).

I see your point now. That's a convincing enough argument to me that automatic retries make sense. (I looked into it, and it's straightforward to set up.) Thanks for bearing with me.

> I am skeptical. It will certainly reduce persistent failures, which is good, but everyone currently runs TryBot and yet they flake.

I'm being imprecise in my terminology, apologies. Yeah, agreed that it will primarily impact persistent failures. From the perspective of someone running `longtest` builders, this may appear as a "flake" today (that is worked around by rebasing), and what I'd primarily been thinking about.

Agreed that actual, low-rate flake rates are less likely to change. I do think flakes encountered in presubmit tend to have a higher priority and get fixed sooner (we can also measure the relative failure rates in CI to get an idea of how different the two are). But if we do automatic retries, that's moot.

> I am not worried about this. Even ignoring the potential security implications of a failing crypto test, I am going to be more disrupted by having to fix a test in a follow-up CL than by a failing Commit-Queue+2, even if it invalidates my approvals, so this is a net positive of Commit-Queue+2.

Fair enough, that comparison makes sense.

> Ah yeah, we probably introduce more than our fair share of persistent longtest failures (because only crypto/tls runs longtest in CQ+1, and because we have probably more -short skips), and Commit-Queue+2 would help with that.

👍

issue.title: x/build: add -longcrypto TryBot

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: NeedsDecision
labels.description: Feedback is required from experts, contributors, and/or the community before a change can be made.

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
Currently, the -longtest TryBots run on CLs that touch crypto/tls, to run the slow-ish BoGo test suite.

Also, cmd/dist runs the `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...` tests only in long mode.

This is sub-optimal in two ways: CLs that touch crypto/tls are excruciatingly slow to TryBot, and flake and suffer from broken builders all the time; meanwhile, most crypto CLs are not tested against older versions of the FIPS module.

Can we add a new TryBot which is enabled for all CLs that touch crypto/... and that tests in long mode only crypto/... and `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...`?

/cc @golang/release @golang/security

comment.body:
> I'm not following: wouldn't we have two cycles of retries (one for TryBots, one for presubmits), which is worse than one?

Let me try to explain what this workflow would look like.

You upload a change to Gerrit.

If it's a small (or no-op) change you already tested locally: you get it reviewed (meet submit requirements, including Googler +1) and you hit Commit-Queue+2 immediately, which will run the builders and if everything passes then great, it submits and you're done.

If it's a larger change and you want to test it against Commit-Queue+1 on more platforms, then you hit Commit-Queue+1. But Commit-Queue+1 is optional. Ultimately you get it reviewed (meet submit requirements, including Googler +1), and you hit Commit-Queue+2, which submits for you.

If you hit an unrelated flake in Commit-Queue+1, that's no different than today.
If you hit an unrelated flake in Commit-Queue+2, _you still have all your submit requirements_. Once you realize what's wrong, you can just hit Commit-Queue+2 again, which is also no different from today.

Does that help clarify where I'm coming from a little bit? I'm specifically trying to say, at worst, even if we somehow get no benefit in builder reliability, it should at least be the same as today.

> The retries are painful for me because they require context switching, and because they turn a CL with Code-Review+2 Auto-Submit+1 from a done task to an open TODO that requires checking in, reading the Gerrit emails to learn if we got a Code-Review+1 or a TryBot-Result-1, etc. (I might have more attention and executive function issues than the average developer, making this a bigger problem for me than it is for others, I acknowledge.)

Totally, I'm 100% on board with reducing retries. Interrupts are bad.

My hope is that running more tests before we reach CI globally (via Commit-Queue+2 running `longtest` builders), that we shift the flakiness of those builders left for _everyone_, making it culturally much more likely that those builders stay clean. The core problem I see with only a subset of the project running `longtest` before a CL is submitted (like crypto and cmd/go) is they bear the brunt of flakiness introduced by others. I think the data I dug up from failing builds on `longtest` supports that hypothesis (>70% of `longtest` build failures weren't related to the two parts of the project that actually run `longtest` early).

And the idea is that it's not too much of a drain on everyone's time to run these heavier-weight builders before the CL lands, because for most it only runs in the final step. Commit-Queue+2 is supposed to be set-and-forget (including for whole stacks of changes) provided there aren't any issues with the CL and there aren't any flakes.

Simultaneously, my expectation is that, even if I'm wrong about a cultural left-shift, retries due to unrelated flakes should at least be _no worse_ than before, by my explanation above.

> To be clear, the flakes I am worried about are "true" flakes: unrelated tests that sometimes fail for unrelated reasons. A test that fails because of the CL is not a flake, it's a bug, and catching it is worth whatever friction it will take (at least to crypto/...).

I agree that related tests that fail are less of an issue, but it can be annoying to have to go back to the drawing board after you got all the submit requirements. To avoid going back and forth with Commit-Queue+2 in this case (because Commit-Queue+2 requires submit requirements) and if it runs a superset of builders compared to Commit-Queue+1, the right thing to do is to manually add the failing builder (like `longtest`) to your opt-in set in the commit message (via a `Cq-Include-Tryjobs` footer, documented at https://go.dev/wiki/SlowBots). Either that, or if this becomes _really_ annoying, then we add those Commit-Queue+2 builders to the regular set for just specific parts of the project (like we do today).

> I am not sure what the ratio of non-crypto, non-cmd/go failures represents, but also I am surprised to hear crypto/... has flaking tests. Is there an easy way for me to query them? (Also, I'd be happy to get cc'd on any watchflakes hit under crypto/..., although most of them look like infra issues: network failures or timeouts.)

Sorry, I should have said that this also includes persistent failures. I just looked back at the last 450 failing builds (further and we lost some history due to retention policy) and what categorized what caused them to fail. I suspect many of the crypto failures are related to persistent FIPS140-related failures that slipped into CI (the failing packages were all FIPS related). I edited my previous comments to make that clearer.

issue.body:
Currently, the -longtest TryBots run on CLs that touch crypto/tls, to run the slow-ish BoGo test suite.

Also, cmd/dist runs the `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...` tests only in long mode.

This is sub-optimal in two ways: CLs that touch crypto/tls are excruciatingly slow to TryBot, and flake and suffer from broken builders all the time; meanwhile, most crypto CLs are not tested against older versions of the FIPS module.

Can we add a new TryBot which is enabled for all CLs that touch crypto/... and that tests in long mode only crypto/... and `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...`?

/cc @golang/release @golang/security

comment.body:
That last query only normalized once per build per package, this one also normalizes per category (so if in a single build, all crypto package failures for example are treated as a single instance of a failure). This is a little more fair and significantly deflates the crypto package flakes, since it seems that if one crypto package failed, a lot of other crypto packages would fail too.

```
bb ls -n 450 -status failure -json -fields=infra golang/ci/gotip-linux-amd64-longtest | jq -r '.infra.resultdb.invocation' | sed 's/invocations\///' | rdb query -u -json | jq -r '[.invocationId, .testResult.testId] | @csv' | sed 's/\.Test[^"]*//' | sort | uniq | sed 's/,".*crypto.*"/crypto/' | sed 's/,".*cmd\/go.*"/cmd\/go/' | sed 's/,".*runtime.*"/runtime/' | sed 's/,".*"/other/' | sed 's/.*,.*//' | grep -v "^\$" | sort | uniq | sed 's/".*"//' | sort | uniq -c

     92 cmd/go
     40 crypto
    200 other
    139 runtime
```

This just supports my hypothesis more. The ratio is more like >70% of ~flakes~ build failures are non-crypto, non-cmd/go.

issue.body:
Currently, the -longtest TryBots run on CLs that touch crypto/tls, to run the slow-ish BoGo test suite.

Also, cmd/dist runs the `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...` tests only in long mode.

comment.body:
I looked at at the last 450 failing builds in CI of `gotip-linux-amd64-longtest` and found that my hypothesis is mostly true?

```
$ bb ls -n 450 -status failure -json -fields=infra golang/ci/gotip-linux-amd64-longtest | jq -r '.infra.resultdb.invocation' | sed 's/invocations\///' | rdb query -u -json | jq -r '[.invocationId, .testResult.testId] | @csv' | sed 's/\.Test[^"]*//' | sort | uniq | sed 's/"[^,]*,//' | sed 's/.*crypto.*/crypto/' | sed 's/.*cmd\/go.*/cmd\/go/' | sed 's/.*runtime.*/runtime/' | sed 's/".*"/other/'| sort | uniq -c

     92 cmd/go
    203 crypto
    201 other
    179 runtime
```

So >50% of ~flakes~ failures on `longtest` have nothing to do with the two main areas (crypto, cmd/go) running `longtest` in presubmit. That being said, `cmd/go` and `crypto` packages are both disproportionately represented, but so is `runtime`, where do NOT run `longtest`.

issue.body:
### Go version

`go version go1.26-devel_e88be8a Sun Nov 23 09:07:32 2025 -0800 linux/amd64`

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
Workspace is `go.godbolt.org` on `x86-64 gc (tip)` as above.
```

### What did you do?

[Code+output tested [here](https://go.godbolt.org/#z:%2BHQCOEHRAQjaQv4qgBPTh65PMYipgDyIW0TWdeqDbEEuYYtHTLtIWBCzmA9jEtAd3F4WBYhnDiwV%2BBJzROmFbCswqiazljGd4YJKNtoeBCxDTjiwNoIxDwLCnpEHxABShZmE7RnnRkN0yoBmACgAanhMD9c9FGNv%2BIIRGJ2FIZIJFCp1DWuhDAYZ4mPoC4OpA0yoNEZStrwqC7huWCQRA0yNM0NgQHYAzuFwXi%2BP4nR5N0HhDOkCQCLhEheBRZSjF0ERkcUpQtH0NROHUNEsUWbEjERYykUMCjsdR%2BHDNUDEkUxXDoRqCwSBK0rWjWSocFsqgABwAGwALTaZIWzAJ8ECRsCEC4IQ9zarqky8M6WiTMaprmpwVqkFuZqkHKCpqfajr6uebqehASBBiG/oUBA4V%2BhEVQsAZAD64aRtGEZxpQiY1lmabbjlOb5oWljbqWjAEBWVY2nWDZNrQLbbh2XbiDWfa8YOw68KO46Tm25CCLONbzouy4YD29kbluvW7vumCHse4b%2BKALoXlet73o%2Bz5ynqb7CKI4jfjtf5qDauheCBximENqHQbBiTwYqSF4Ch8DoSUvFYThnGDN4DDoFJ4zkXElFJF9eExED9ECYxegYWUrT9KDMNvZh5Tsf93TCaJiOY/xOTQ7JMxzApBMWjK3k2mpWwHAASgAsgZRkmWZIKWUQxBbDZsn2ee0wIJgTBYBEaH6G5vCeVEPkIXaZgBQ5RqkCaXkWgUKm%2BdLctOSLHAeKrUscNzy3TLu8Q2JIQA%3D%3D)]

Compile this simplified example:

```go
var someError error

func f(w bool) (int, int, int, error) {
	if w {
		return 0, 0, 0, someError
	}
	return 1, 2, 1, nil
}
```

### What did you see happen?

The output for `0, 0, 0` above uses `XORL` and two `MOVQ`:
```asm
        XORL    AX, AX
        MOVQ    AX, BX
        MOVQ    AX, CX
```

### What did you expect to see?

All zeroing of registers here should use ` XORL`.

issue.title: internal/cpu,golang.org/x/sys/cpu: support windows/arm64

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: FeatureRequest

labels.name: compiler/runtime

labels.name: LibraryProposal
labels.description: Issues describing a requested change to the Go standard library or x/ libraries, but not to a tool

issue.body:
This is a tracking issue to add support for Windows ARM64 to `internal/cpu` and `golang.org/x/sys/cpu`. They currently don't set any supported cpu flag on this platform, even when Windows provides an API to prove if a given feature is supported by the current computer: [IsProcessorFeaturePresent](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/processthreadsapi/nf-processthreadsapi-isprocessorfeaturepresent).

This is a mapping of Go feature flags to IsProcessorFeaturePresent flags (note that some flags are taken from the published Windows headers, as learn.microsoft.com is not really up-to-date).

- HasAES => PF_ARM_V8_CRYPTO_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasPMULL => PF_ARM_V8_CRYPTO_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSHA1 => PF_ARM_V8_CRYPTO_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSHA2 => PF_ARM_V8_CRYPTO_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSHA3 => PF_ARM_SHA3_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE 
- HasCRC32 => PF_ARM_V8_CRC32_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSHA512 => PF_ARM_SHA512_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasATOMICS => PF_ARM_V81_ATOMIC_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasASIMDDP => PF_ARM_V82_DP_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasASIMDRDM => PF_ARM_V82_DP_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasLRCPC => PF_ARM_V83_LRCPC_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSM3=> PF_ARM_V83_LRCPC_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSVE => PF_ARM_SVE_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSVE2 => PF_ARM_SVE2_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasJSCVT => PF_ARM_V83_JSCVT_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE

@golang/windows

comment.body: CC @golang/windows @golang/runtime

issue.body:
This is a tracking issue to add support for Windows ARM64 to `internal/cpu` and `golang.org/x/sys/cpu`. They currently don't set any supported cpu flag on this platform, even when Windows provides an API to prove if a given feature is supported by the current computer: [IsProcessorFeaturePresent](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/processthreadsapi/nf-processthreadsapi-isprocessorfeaturepresent).

This is a mapping of Go feature flags to IsProcessorFeaturePresent flags (note that some flags are taken from the published Windows headers, as learn.microsoft.com is not really up-to-date).

- HasAES => PF_ARM_V8_CRYPTO_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasPMULL => PF_ARM_V8_CRYPTO_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSHA1 => PF_ARM_V8_CRYPTO_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSHA2 => PF_ARM_V8_CRYPTO_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSHA3 => PF_ARM_SHA3_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE 
- HasCRC32 => PF_ARM_V8_CRC32_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSHA512 => PF_ARM_SHA512_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasATOMICS => PF_ARM_V81_ATOMIC_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasASIMDDP => PF_ARM_V82_DP_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasASIMDRDM => PF_ARM_V82_DP_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasLRCPC => PF_ARM_V83_LRCPC_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSM3=> PF_ARM_V83_LRCPC_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSVE => PF_ARM_SVE_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasSVE2 => PF_ARM_SVE2_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE
- HasJSCVT => PF_ARM_V83_JSCVT_INSTRUCTIONS_AVAILABLE

issue.title: proposal: sync: new type `MapHash`

labels.name: LibraryProposal
labels.description: Issues describing a requested change to the Go standard library or x/ libraries, but not to a tool

milestone.title: Proposal
milestone.description:
Proposals that are pending (not yet accepted/rejected).
See https://go.dev/s/proposal-process.

issue.body:
# proposal: sync: new type `MapHash`

## Background

The sync package's `Map` type treats keys and values as type `any`, and requires that the keys are comparable. This both leaves performance on the table by frequently forcing simple value types to be boxed into an `any` and eschews type-safety, leading to lots of code that simply states the types of the keys and values in a comment, such as this (one of many) example from the standard library:

```go
var listCache sync.Map // map[string]listImports, keyed by contextName
```

Today we have generics, so we can make uses of `sync.Map` (which are the common ones) much more type-safe. The current situation is especially silly since the underlying implementation of a `Map` has actually been type-parameterized since Go 1.23!

One reason we haven't been able to move forward with a type-parameterized `sync.Map` in the `sync` package itself is that we had [no idea what to call it](https://github.com/golang/go/discussions/48287). [Creating a new `sync/v2` package](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/71076) was proposed as an alternative, but because only two types (`Pool` and `Map`) would actually benefit from generics at this time, we would be forced to incur all the costs of a `v2` package for just those types.

Also, @bradfitz has since [exported the internal implementation](https://github.com/go4org/hashtriemap) of `sync.Map`, `internal/sync.HashTrieMap`.

## Proposal

With the [standardization of the hash function](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/70471), there's an opportunity to modernize the `Map` type and give it a new, clear name: `MapHash`. The existing `Map` can be defined in terms of it.

I propose the following API changes for the `sync` package.

```go
type MapHash[K, V any, H maphash.Hasher[K]] struct{ ... }

func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) All() iter.Seq2[K, V]
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Clear()
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) CompareAndDelete(key K, old V) (deleted bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) CompareAndSwap(key K, old, new V) (swapped bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Delete(key K)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Load(key K) (value V, ok bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) LoadAndDelete(key K) (value V, loaded bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) LoadOrStore(key K, value V) (result V, loaded bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Range(yield func(key K, value V) bool) // for sync.Map
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Store(key K, old V)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Swap(key K, new V) (previous V, loaded bool)

type Map = MapHash[any, any, maphash.ComparableHasher[any]]
```

This API is identical to `sync.Map` except that it makes use of the type parameters, and adds an idiomatic iterator with an `All` method that returns an `iter.Seq2`. This method has the same synchronization guarantees as `Range`.

**NOTE**: Do not pay too close attention to the type signature. It will change to follow whatever the broader consensus is on container type signatures. For now, this proposal simply matches #69559. The core part of the proposal is the addition of a new type with a custom `maphash.Hasher` in the existing sync package as a way to move forward on having at type-safe concurrent map. Changes to this part may affect some of the minor details in this proposal, such as whether we replace `Map` with a type alias.

### Rationale: Why a custom `maphash.Hasher`?

The use-case for a custom `maphash.Hasher` is admittedly niche. Most of the time a `comparable` key type is good enough. The downside of requiring a `maphash.Hasher` is that it complicates the API.

There are two reasons I propose supporting a custom hash function:
1. Consistency across the standard library.
2. The underlying implementation trivially supports it.

For (1), this API is intended to exist alongside https://github.com/golang/go/issues/69559, which proposes a map implementation with a custom hash function.

For (2), my concern is that eliding support for custom hash functions would just encourage [more unnecessary exports](https://github.com/go4org/hashtriemap) of the internal map implementation, precisely _because_ it can trivially support it. This makes the choice of concurrent map even more messy and complicated in the broader ecosystem. A good quality implementation already exists in the standard library.

### Alternative: Don't redefine `Map`

Alternatively, we don't define `Map` as a type alias, and instead just use the `MapHash` implementation under the hood. This way we don't need to add the `Range` method to `MapHash`.

One benefit of the type alias approach is that it may minimize the visibility of `Map` in the documentation when users go looking for a concurrent map.

### Optional: Shorthand for comparable keys

A downside of `MapHash` is that common cases are very verbose, requiring the developer to write out `maphash.ComparableHasher` everywhere the type is referenced. We can make this common case much less verbose with a generic type alias, too:

```go
type MapOf[K comparable, V any] = MapHash[K, V, maphash.ComparableHasher[K]]
```

The `MapOf` name is not intended to set a precedent or a new pattern that others should follow. It's a compromise that may or may not be a better alternative to creating a new `sync/v2` package.

## Implementation 

We would need to rework the underlying map implementation of `internal/sync.HashTrieMap` to use the new `maphash.Hasher`, but this is fairly trivial to do, since the equality and hash functions are already invoked indirectly. Then we would be able to refactor `internal/sync.HashTrieMap` into the implementation of `sync.MapHash` in the `sync` package and remove the `internal/sync` package, which would be a nice internal cleanup.

## Go 1 Compatibility

This proposal only adds a new API, so it's backward compatible. Although `sync.Map` is redefined, it still exposes an identical API to today, with the sole addition of the `All` method.

## Alternatives

As mentioned earlier, we could choose to pursue a `sync/v2` package wholesale instead, for which [a proposal](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/71076) already exists.

I don't feel strongly about either, but at this point the `sync/v2` proposal has fallen behind a little bit (namely around the addition of `maphash.Hasher`), though it could be trivially updated to accommodate it.

## Acknowledgements

Thank you to Cherry, David, and Russ for your initial feedback on this proposal.

comment.body:
> I guess the question is: Why must a v2 package require a substantial redesign?

It doesn't, it just historically has, since it's a brand new package. For me, it's less about what's true in a raw technical sense and how it's perceived by humans and the Go community.

IMO, perceptually, a `v2` is just always going to appear to people as a chance to "do it right," and if we don't make enough changes, "a missed opportunity." There are already comments on the `sync/v2` issue with suggestions to tweak other APIs here and there. Sure, we can choose to ignore those comments, but I think it does add a whole lot of friction to the process because it makes identifying a consensus harder.

RE: deferring more contentious changes to a `v3`, I think that's so far in the future that I'm not even sure how to reason about it. For me, a `sync/v2` is borderline because it's caught up in the monumental language change that generics was in Go 1.18. We're _still_ figuring out the ramifications of the generics design, as evidenced by the fact that we don't know what container types even really look like yet. To me, this is just stronger evidence that we should be focusing on adding types to v1 `sync` (or in a new package altogether). If it's not quite right (though obviously we should endeavor to get it right the first time), we still have the `v2` and the justification of not having quite figured out the right way to express standard library APIs with generics yet to clean up the warts and "do it right this time."

There's also plenty of prior art to adding generic APIs to existing packages with suboptimal names. For example, we didn't make a `reflect/v2` just for `TypeFor`! That wasn't nearly as contentious as `sync.MapHash` seems to be.

Coming back to `v3`, I would expect a `v3` to require, at baseline, a similarly monumental language change to justify it. I have no idea what form that would take, or even if such a monumental language change will ever happen again.

This is just my current perspective, perhaps others (on the Go team or otherwise) disagree.
