**NOTE**: Do not pay too close attention to the type signature. It will change to follow whatever the broader consensus is on container type signatures. For now, this proposal simply matches #69559. The core part of the proposal is the addition of a new type with a custom `maphash.Hasher` in the existing sync package as a way to move forward on having at type-safe concurrent map. Changes to this part may affect some of the minor details in this proposal, such as whether we replace `Map` with a type alias.

### Rationale: Why a custom `maphash.Hasher`?

The use-case for a custom `maphash.Hasher` is admittedly niche. Most of the time a `comparable` key type is good enough. The downside of requiring a `maphash.Hasher` is that it complicates the API.

There are two reasons I propose supporting a custom hash function:
1. Consistency across the standard library.
2. The underlying implementation trivially supports it.

For (1), this API is intended to exist alongside https://github.com/golang/go/issues/69559, which proposes a map implementation with a custom hash function.

For (2), my concern is that eliding support for custom hash functions would just encourage [more unnecessary exports](https://github.com/go4org/hashtriemap) of the internal map implementation, precisely _because_ it can trivially support it. This makes the choice of concurrent map even more messy and complicated in the broader ecosystem. A good quality implementation already exists in the standard library.

### Alternative: Don't redefine `Map`

Alternatively, we don't define `Map` as a type alias, and instead just use the `MapHash` implementation under the hood. This way we don't need to add the `Range` method to `MapHash`.

One benefit of the type alias approach is that it may minimize the visibility of `Map` in the documentation when users go looking for a concurrent map.

### Optional: Shorthand for comparable keys

A downside of `MapHash` is that common cases are very verbose, requiring the developer to write out `maphash.ComparableHasher` everywhere the type is referenced. We can make this common case much less verbose with a generic type alias, too:

```go
type MapOf[K comparable, V any] = MapHash[K, V, maphash.ComparableHasher[K]]
```

The `MapOf` name is not intended to set a precedent or a new pattern that others should follow. It's a compromise that may or may not be a better alternative to creating a new `sync/v2` package.

## Implementation 

We would need to rework the underlying map implementation of `internal/sync.HashTrieMap` to use the new `maphash.Hasher`, but this is fairly trivial to do, since the equality and hash functions are already invoked indirectly. Then we would be able to refactor `internal/sync.HashTrieMap` into the implementation of `sync.MapHash` in the `sync` package and remove the `internal/sync` package, which would be a nice internal cleanup.

## Go 1 Compatibility

This proposal only adds a new API, so it's backward compatible. Although `sync.Map` is redefined, it still exposes an identical API to today, with the sole addition of the `All` method.

## Alternatives

As mentioned earlier, we could choose to pursue a `sync/v2` package wholesale instead, for which [a proposal](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/71076) already exists.

I don't feel strongly about either, but at this point the `sync/v2` proposal has fallen behind a little bit (namely around the addition of `maphash.Hasher`), though it could be trivially updated to accommodate it.

## Acknowledgements

Thank you to Cherry, David, and Russ for your initial feedback on this proposal.

comment.body:
> If we proceed by adding generic variants alongside existing APIs, we effectively lock that expanded surface in forever.

The root of my argument is: this is true if we add a `v2` as well, except we're locking a _much larger_ duplicated API surface, just to avoid what is a naming problem for two types. All the other points of discussion we've encountered so far, I argue, would have come up in a `sync/v2` discussion as well.

I don't think it's obvious which one has a higher cost long-term because we simply don't have that much experience with `v2` packages yet. We've added new types to existing packages plenty of times before, but we've only done a `v2` a handful of times. In the case of `encoding/json/v2` and `math/rand/v2`, they were rethinking the packages from the ground up. Clearly over the threshold for a `v2`.

Because every `v2` so far was a ground-up rethink of the v1 package, I worry that a `sync/v2` opens the door to that. I think we're pretty sure we don't want to touch the rest of `sync` right now because there are bigger fish to fry.

From another perspective, I don't personally feel that I have the bandwidth to champion an entire `v2` to package right now (again, looking at how much effort that required historically) and argue why it's useful and necessary, but I do have the bandwidth to champion a proposal to add one type to an existing package. I think that alone, in my mind, pushes me away from a `v2` and is at least some evidence that a `v2` is overkill.

I think coming up with a couple names that everyone is OK with for a niche type is not that bad. I don't think it will make a significant difference in every day Go code whether someone sees `sync.MapHash` instead of `sync.Map`. Those four characters (ignoring the type arguments, which a `v2` would also have) aren't worth much for such a niche type. I don't think there will be much confusion in the documentation in practice. One is clearly generic, the other is not; type aliases could also help with the transition, and we could have a third type for `comparable` keys that doesn't require a constructor for drop-in replacement and type safety.

Many voices in this issue are in favor of `sync/v2`, but I would hazard a guess that most Go developers are just in favor of having a generic `sync.Map` at all, `v2` or not. Again, a `v2` _by default_ opens a much bigger can of worms, looking at our experience with them. Even if we add this type to `sync` and decide it was a mistake, we can still do a `v2` later! This is not true of doing a `v2` now, especially while we're still figuring out container types in general.

And also, just IMO, there's really so little reason we shouldn't have a public generic map type already. The implementation exists, and the technical work was done long ago. I think we've established that there are decisions to be made about how hash container types are going to work _in general_, but that's somewhat outside the scope of this proposal, so this proposal (and a `v2`, too) are blocked on that anyway. But that aside, all we're missing is at this point is a name, and a couple decisions around a `comparable` version of the type (which I'm positive would come up in a `sync/v2` discussion too). The bit about a custom hash function is just about alignment with future std container types and a way to come up with a reasonable name.

Finally, I _do_ think this case is ambiguous. I want to be clear that Ii everyone wants a `v2`, then I'm not at all opposed to that, but I do think it's too big a hammer for the size of problem here and someone else is going to have to carry on that proposal if there's to be any movement on this in the near term (which is of course fine, Ian filed the `sync/v2` proposal in the first place, but I just want to be clear that arguing a `v2` is better isn't going to change my priorities, unfortunately).

issue.title: runtime, cmd/compile: add runtime.freegc and runtime.freegcTracked to reduce GC work (GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc)

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: Implementation
labels.description: Issues describing a semantics-preserving change to the Go implementation.

issue.body:
**UPDATE** (Nov 27, 2025): 

* The design doc is now published in the Go proposal repo: https://go.dev/design/74299-runtime-freegc
* Merged CLs:
   * [runtime: add GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefree](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/700235)
   * [runtime: set GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc to disabled by default](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/720120)
   * [runtime: add runtime.freegc to reduce GC work](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/673695)
   * [runtime: add more precise test of assist credit handling for runtime.freegc](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/717520)
   * [runtime: support runtime.freegc in size-specialized mallocs for noscan objects](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/715761)
   * [cmd/compile: introduce alias analysis and automatically free non-aliased memory after growslice](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/710015)

Additional CLs are in progress, including some additional compiler-focused ones.   
   
The best place to start is probably the "Introduction" section of the [design doc](https://go.dev/design/74299-runtime-freegc). There are also some sample performance results in that document.

The remainder of this comment below here is from ~June, 2025, and is probably less interesting at this point than the design doc.

----

Opening this issue here to help with discussion.

### Background

[CL 673695](https://go.dev/cl/673695) starts with:

> For reducing allocations, there is sometimes a chicken and egg problem.
> 
> For example, #72036 is a WIP approach to update escape analysis so that interface arguments do not always escape, with a primary target of w.Write(buf) and friends, but many interesting byte slices are variable size known only at runtime. Currently, a make with an unknown capacity (beyond 32 bytes) causes a slice to be heap allocated, so solely improving escape analysis in this case has limited impact on actual applications -- if a slice is heap allocated for two reasons, eliminating one reason just means it is still heap allocated for the other reason, which lessens the value of doing the work to eliminate the first reason.
> 
> In an attempt to help triangulate on what is possible for the runtime, compiler, and standard library to do together to reduce how much work the GC must do, this CL has a proof-of-concept set of runtime.free implementations

Back in February, as part of my write up for #72036 I mentioned that a runtime.free could help with this chicken and egg problem:

> I have a mostly working runtime.free that can be inserted by the compiler (and not for use by normal Go code of course), and I'm looking at hooking it up a few different ways, including when a slice is both variable size and also used as an interface method arg. (I'll probably open a separate issue on this, but I'm also attempting to free memory in some runtime map growth cases, some append cases, etc.

### Prototype

[CL 673695](https://go.dev/cl/673695) contains that mostly working runtime.free. (It's "mostly working" in the sense that it can pass its own tests, including I let it accumulate a few hundred CPU hours running its tests successfully under `stress`. That of course does not mean it is bug free, and it still has some shortcuts, including for example how it updates user-facing stats).

I also have some additional WIP CLs that update the compiler to automatically insert free calls for slices allocated via `make` when the compiler can prove it is safe to do so, and separately updating maps to free map storage during growth and split as well as a small number of lower-level stdlib things like `strings.Builder` and `bytes.Buffer` (which are "low level" in the sense that they already encapsulate some low-level performance tricks, including use of unsafe, but also they let other higher-level pieces of the stdlib then safely benefit from their performance). It is not intended that higher-level portions of the stdlib (like net/http) would have manual calls to a runtime.free.

Patterns of `append` usage like `slices.Collect` and `slices.AppendSeq` are also targets, with some good initial results. (`slices.Collect` for example could be updated manually to start, which I've tried as an experiment, or it could wait for the compiler to automatically recognize its pattern).

Note that runtime.free and the related runtime entry points are not public APIs, and cannot be called directly by end users.

### Basic approach

See the more detailed write-up in the [CL 673695](https://go.dev/cl/673695) commit message, which covers these 4 runtime entry points to start:
```
 func free(ptr unsafe.Pointer)
 func freesized(ptr unsafe.Pointer, uintptr size, noscan bool)
 func freetracked(trackedObjs *[]trackedObj)
 func makeslicetracked64(et *_type, len64 int64, cap64 int64, trackedObjs *[]trackedObj) unsafe.Pointer
```

At least for the approach I have so far, the free work tries to take advantage of things the compiler already knows or that the compiler is taught with modest effort (for example in escape analysis).

### Performance

Some preliminary benchmarks. See [CL 673695](https://go.dev/cl/673695) for commentary on these benchmarks.

#### strings.Builder.Write with runtime.freesized

```
                                               │       old        │    new                              │
                                               │      sec/op      │   sec/op     vs base                │
BuildString_Builder/1Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           55.82n ± 2%   55.86n ± 2%        ~ (p=0.794 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/2Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           125.2n ± 2%   115.4n ± 1%   -7.86% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/3Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           224.0n ± 1%   188.2n ± 2%  -16.00% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/5Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           239.1n ± 9%   205.1n ± 1%  -14.20% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/8Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           422.8n ± 3%   325.4n ± 1%  -23.04% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/10Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4          436.9n ± 2%   342.3n ± 1%  -21.64% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/100Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4         4.403µ ± 1%   2.381µ ± 2%  -45.91% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/1000Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4        48.28µ ± 2%   21.38µ ± 2%  -55.71% (p=0.000 n=20)
```

(In short, currently `strings.Builder` incrementally builds up an internal buffer, growing as needed. The modification introduced N-1 frees for N allocations, with the final allocation going to the user. There is only 1 allocation if there is only 1 `Builder.Write` call, so there is no benefit seen on the first line, but the benefit grows to ~2x faster as there are more allocations per benchmark loop).

#### Normal allocation (with these changes, but no free/reuse happening)

The new reuse code path for reusing a freed object is currently somewhat interwoven with the normal allocation path in runtime/malloc.go, but some initial micro-benchmarks suggest the impact of these changes on the normal allocation path when no reuse is happening could be small to negligible:


```
                        │         old        │  new                             │
                        │        sec/op      │ sec/op     vs base               │
Malloc8-16                      11.01n ± 1%   10.94n ± 1%  -0.68% (p=0.038 n=20)
Malloc16-16                     17.15n ± 1%   17.05n ± 0%  -0.55% (p=0.007 n=20)
Malloc32-16                     18.65n ± 1%   18.42n ± 0%  -1.26% (p=0.000 n=20)
MallocTypeInfo8-16              18.63n ± 0%   18.36n ± 0%  -1.45% (p=0.000 n=20)
MallocTypeInfo16-16             22.32n ± 0%   22.65n ± 0%  +1.50% (p=0.000 n=20)
MallocTypeInfo32-16             23.37n ± 0%   23.89n ± 0%  +2.23% (p=0.000 n=20)
geomean                         18.02n        18.01n       -0.05%
```

The initial performance seems promising, but still preliminary.

In general, some possible performance benefits might include:

 1. less CPU used by GC, which might matter most when CPU is constrained or exhausted (either on a sustained basis, or over some shorter but meaningful time interval).
 2. longer time between GCs, with less time overall with the write barrier enabled (helping application code execute faster).
 3. more cache-friendly allocs for user code (esp. if it ends up with LIFO or near-LIFO free/alloc).
 4. with less GC work overall, maybe fewer assists or other ways the GC can intrude on the app.
 5. maybe slightly more friendly to the new Green Tea GC if it can result in a higher density per span (speculation 😅).

The first maybe is the most obvious, but that might not justify the change for apps that have spare CPU and the GC CPU costs are almost entirely in the "background" compared to the rest of the app work.

The third is particularly interesting because it might help make it more of a win in CPU costs even before considering the costs of running the GC. (I suspect I've observed this effect in some of the benchmarks, but need to look more carefully).

One approach to mitigate the cost for applications that don't care much about GC costs today is the free-related work currently has a size-based threshold (currently 16 bytes). That could be raised to something higher than 16 to the point where the other costs of allocating (including usually zeroing) or operating on the result (like writing data and doing useful work) are large enough costs compared to the overhead of the free-related work such that the free-related overhead is small enough. (For compiler-inserted frees of slices, if the threshold does need to increase from 16, it would be nice if the free threshold could be low enough to match the 32-byte threshold for new Go 1.25 stack allocation optimization introduced by Keith, or maybe that 32-byte threshold ends up being higher). Also, the current cost in the prototype likely can be made cheaper.

Another approach to manage the costs could be via PGO, though I'm hopeful a first version could be viable without requiring PGO.

That said, it likely might be easier to sort through some of the performance trade-offs by seeing more concrete performance examples, which is one of the main goals of this current prototype.

---------

There are multiple ways that runtime.free and related entry points could be used, but I'm hoping this issue could be focused on some possible first steps.

In any event, this is still exploratory. @mknyszek took a look at the code a ~month ago and gave some helpful initial feedback. Absent someone pointing out a fatal flaw in the overall approach, I plan to try to polish things up some more and get more feedback. It might be that this exact approach does not pan out, but maybe we'll learn something via the exploration.

comment.body:
> So, can it be used to make feature ownership like rust？

Probably not. Rust's ownership model relies on a lot of type system machinery that I suspect would be infeasible to track automatically through Go's semantics and the way they're utilized in typical Go programs. In theory, maybe, but for now this is just an experiment (and like @thepudds said, still in relatively early stages, `GOEXPERIMENT` doesn't automatically mean it's ready for feedback). We think this is a promising direction but please do not get too excited about it just yet.

issue.title: x/build: add -longcrypto TryBot

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: NeedsDecision
labels.description: Feedback is required from experts, contributors, and/or the community before a change can be made.

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
Currently, the -longtest TryBots run on CLs that touch crypto/tls, to run the slow-ish BoGo test suite.

Also, cmd/dist runs the `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...` tests only in long mode.

This is sub-optimal in two ways: CLs that touch crypto/tls are excruciatingly slow to TryBot, and flake and suffer from broken builders all the time; meanwhile, most crypto CLs are not tested against older versions of the FIPS module.

Can we add a new TryBot which is enabled for all CLs that touch crypto/... and that tests in long mode only crypto/... and `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...`?

/cc @golang/release @golang/security

comment.body:
> One thing I am very worried about, though, is flakiness. If (1) is just turned on today with no automatic retries, it will make the experience of landing changes even more painful, and I am not convinced that pain will translate to less flakiness, because in my experience the landing experience has been getting worse over the years.

That's a good point about not making the overall experience worse.

FWIW, I think if there's flakiness at the end of the Gerrit workflow that's specifically _not associated with the CL to be merged_, then that should be no worse than today. Commit-Queue+2 just moves where the retries happen, since you won't lose the Googler +1s when re-running Commit-Queue+2.

However, if the flakiness _does_ involve the CL in question, yeah, that forces a full round-trip which can be painful. We may want to consider running longtest early anyway for some parts of the codebase that are far reaching, or just have a lot of the test suite in longtest, to avoid a full round trip through the Googler +1 queue, in which case we're back to longtest builder speed being important (or we add the longcrypto variant).

I can look into automatic retries, but I worry they might just be a patch on the problem. If too many flakes overlap, the problems just manifest later, once N retries fail. I think what needs to change is the baseline flakiness of longtest. LUCI has a much more sophisticated feature called test exoneration which will mark failing tests as "known failing" allowing the merge to proceed. This doesn't have the same issue of the problem manifesting later, but is much more complex to set up, and is primarily designed for Chromium's scale (their commit rate is way higher, and their builders take much longer to run). I'm reluctant to set that up for our scale, IMO it should be feasible to try and make our test suite less flaky just via culture.

Specifically, my hope is that after everyone's full on the new Commit-Queue+2 flow, that longtest will get less flaky simply because it will be in everyone's face. My hypothesis is that many longtest flakes manifest from commits that don't run longtest at all. (Not all certainly, but many.) I think (?) we do a pretty decent job of keeping non-longtest presubmit less flaky, because the chance that a flake is caught during development is much higher, and this probability will increase for longtest flakes with Commit-Queue+2. I can test this hypothesis, I think, by just looking at all longtest test failures and classifying them as either crypto-related, cmd/go-related, or something else.

The test-with-CL-rebased with Commit-Queue+2 will also improve baseline reliability of all builders a little bit, since there's a smaller chance of a bad merge of a CL based on an old commit.

issue.title: x/build: add -longcmdgo TryBot

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: NeedsDecision

milestone.title: Unreleased

issue.body:
(This issue is inspired by #76768: it's basically longcrypto but for the go command)

We run longtests for CLs affecting files in the cmd/go tree, because many of the cmd/go tests are not run in short mode (for instance because they run builds). But running all the longtests is really slow.

I'd like for us to add a longcmdgo TryBot that runs cmd/go/... tests without -short, but runs the rest of the tests in short mode.

cc @golang/release

comment.body: Relevant comment in the `-longcrypto` request: https://github.com/golang/go/issues/76768#issuecomment-3716696561.

issue.title: x/build: add -longcrypto TryBot

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: NeedsDecision
labels.description: Feedback is required from experts, contributors, and/or the community before a change can be made.

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
Currently, the -longtest TryBots run on CLs that touch crypto/tls, to run the slow-ish BoGo test suite.

Also, cmd/dist runs the `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...` tests only in long mode.

This is sub-optimal in two ways: CLs that touch crypto/tls are excruciatingly slow to TryBot, and flake and suffer from broken builders all the time; meanwhile, most crypto CLs are not tested against older versions of the FIPS module.

Can we add a new TryBot which is enabled for all CLs that touch crypto/... and that tests in long mode only crypto/... and `GOFIPS140=$VERSION go test crypto/...`?

/cc @golang/release @golang/security

comment.body:
I'm not super enthusiastic about adding even more build configurations, especially ones needing more custom logic in `cmd/dist` or in the build script.

Sampling builders a bit, it looks like the `longtest` builders take about 10 minutes longer than regular builds, excluding pending times for shards, which usually occur when there isn't a lot of activity and new machines need to be spun up (but this is also true for the non-`longtest` builders, hence why I exclude it).

It sounds like the problems with `longtest` are that it's too frequently flaky, or that the extra time is too long of a wait. This issue is more about working around those problems than fixing them.

If they're truly insurmountable, then sure, we can go down this path; I don't mind doing the work.

But in an attempt to take a more principled stance, I want to take a moment and try to actually make sure that we're making a decent choice here, and that we can't do something better, even in the short term. Here are some alternatives that target these problems with `longtest` directly:

1. A fix for `longtest` being flaky would be to always run it before submit, on every change. One thing we've been meaning to leverage on the LUCI side is support for Commit-Queue+2, which will run trybots for you and submit whole stacks of changes (without having to set Auto-Submit+1 on the entire stack). We already intend to have a different policy for Commit-Queue+2 (rebase changes before testing), so it may also make sense to 'upgrade' the standard builders to longtest before submitting. This would not slow down the main feedback loop, only submission, and would catch more issues before they hit CI.
2. There hasn't been activity on #26473 in 6 years. I don't think we've done a lot to try to improve test execution times in that time period, which is a _very_ long time.

For (1), I think we should just finally prioritize that. It will get us fully integrated with the LUCI presubmit workflow and finally solve some long-standing build issues. I will look into it this week. This might even be enough. If we start running longtest prior to submission always, then the purpose being to catch issues before submission is moot. The current trybot workflow just ends up being for a quick check and a faster feedback loop, and most of the time they will still (hopefully) be enough.

For (2), in December, I built a tool to construct a pprof profile out of our test results: https://cs.opensource.google/go/x/build/+/master:cmd/resultdbpprof/main.go;drc=46693c1d68709b56071dab871071d38c6a73004c.

I collected profiles from both a regular `gotip-linux-amd64` builder and a `gotip-linux-amd64-longtest` builder:

[gotip-linux-amd64.pb.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24460485/gotip-linux-amd64.pb.gz)
[gotip-linux-amd64-longtest.pb.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24460489/gotip-linux-amd64-longtest.pb.gz)

In the `gotip-linux-amd64-longtest` profile, we spend about 20% of all test execution time in packages containing the string "crypto," so it's already true that a significant chunk of the time in `longtest` is already spent on the crypto tests. (Note: this is a profile, so it doesn't measure latency _within_ packages at all, just total test execution time.) We also spend about 20% of all test execution time in tests matching "cmd/go."

Can we do anything to make the crypto test suite and/or cmd/go test suite faster? Is there anything that sticks out to you in the profile?

I look at the profile and genuinely don't know if there's waste or not. Perhaps everything in there needs to happen in exactly the way that it does, but again, it seems to me like we haven't taken a close look at test execution time in 6 years. I admit I might be completely wrong on that from the crypto side (I know that @matloob has done some work on the cmd/go side, and hit a bit of wall). However, improving test execution time has follow-on benefits. Personally, I would like to take some time this week to improve the test execution time of areas I'm familiar with, like the runtime tests, which is about 10% of the `longtest` execution time. I see some obvious waste there. We run a lot more under `gccheckmark` than we need to, for example. I also know that we build too many binaries in the trace tests, though that only accounts for about 1% of the time.

In terms of test sharding across machines, we're doing an OK job these days (it was much worse earlier last year). Of the 4 shards we spin up for `longtest`, 3 of the builders take 12 minutes to run tests, and one takes 8.4 minutes. That's not a perfect balance, but it's not bad.  Distributing 3 minutes would make the tests take 11 minutes instead of 12 minutes, which would be about 10% better. Good, but not life-changing.

As another alternative, more shards might help. Doubling the number of shards would allow for a best-case improvement of 5.5 minutes, which would make `longtest` closer to `race` in execution time (only about 2 minutes off, excluding pending times), and we run `race` everywhere. We probably wouldn't get all the way there, but perhaps close enough for it to be tolerable for presubmit (literally just before submission).

issue.title: os/signal: Passing bad values to Notify makes Stop hang on Windows

labels.name: Documentation
labels.description: Issues describing a change to documentation.

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
### Go version

go version go1.25.5 windows/amd64
go version go1.24.4 windows/amd64

(others untested)

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
set AR=ar
set CC=gcc
set CGO_CFLAGS=-O2 -g
set CGO_CPPFLAGS=
```

### What did you do?

Ran the following program

```go
package main

import (
        "context"
        "fmt"
        "os"
        "os/signal"
        "sync"

        "golang.org/x/sys/windows"
)

type SignalHandler struct {
        wg     sync.WaitGroup
        cancel context.CancelFunc
}

func (s *SignalHandler) Start() {
        ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
        s.cancel = cancel

        s.wg.Add(1)
        go s.handle(ctx)
}

func (s *SignalHandler) Stop() {
        s.cancel()
        s.wg.Wait()
}

func (s *SignalHandler) handle(ctx context.Context) {
        defer s.wg.Done()

        ch := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
        // 0xaa is some nonsense signal. windows.SIGTERM etc work just as well.
        // Anything but os.Interrupt
        signal.Notify(ch, windows.Signal(0xaa))
        defer signal.Stop(ch)

        for {
                select {
                case <-ctx.Done():
                        return
                case <-ch:
                        continue
                }
        }
}

func main() {
        h := &SignalHandler{}
        fmt.Println("start")
        h.Start()
        fmt.Println("stop")
        h.Stop()
}
```

### What did you see happen?

"start" and "stop" are both printed but the process then hangs. The issue is [signal.Stop](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/f8ee0f84753b22254d217bf28ce8ecca7db7025c/src/runtime/sigqueue.go#L192):

```
0  0x00007ff7a4b83694 in runtime.mcall
   at C:/Program Files/Go/src/runtime/asm_amd64.s:457
1  0x00007ff7a4b7f9f6 in runtime.Gosched
   at C:/Program Files/Go/src/runtime/proc.go:389
2  0x00007ff7a4b7f9f6 in os/signal.signalWaitUntilIdle
   at C:/Program Files/Go/src/runtime/sigqueue.go:193
3  0x00007ff7a4bbf4aa in os/signal.Stop
   at C:/Program Files/Go/src/os/signal/signal.go:215
4  0x00007ff7a4bca1e5 in main.(*SignalHandler).handle.deferwrap2
   at C:/Users/Matt Hall/Documents/test/main.go:36
5  0x00007ff7a4bca157 in main.(*SignalHandler).handle
   at C:/Users/Matt Hall/Documents/test/main.go:42
6  0x00007ff7a4bc9fa8 in main.(*SignalHandler).Start.gowrap1
   at C:/Users/Matt Hall/Documents/test/main.go:23
7  0x00007ff7a4b856e1 in runtime.goexit
   at C:/Program Files/Go/src/runtime/asm_amd64.s:1693
```

Digging around a bit more `sig.state` is stuck in idle. I believe this is because idle is the zero value and `sigsend`/`signal_recv` - which advance the state - are never run.

If I pass `os.Interrupt` to `signal.Notify` instead `signal_recv` is called and the process exists. I can't find where signal_recv is called. The backtrace ends in assembly which looks suspicious:

```
0  0x00007ff76581e94e in os/signal.signal_recv
   at C:/Program Files/Go/src/runtime/sigqueue.go:130
1  0x00007ff76585e9d3 in os/signal.loop
   at C:/Program Files/Go/src/os/signal/signal_unix.go:23
2  0x00007ff765824741 in runtime.goexit
   at C:/Program Files/Go/src/runtime/asm_amd64.s:1693
```

### What did you expect to see?

The process should terminate.

I think the docs could also be clearer here. Reading between the lines it appears the only valid value to be passed on Windows is `os.Interrupted`, but this is not stated outright.

comment.body:
The more I look into it, the more it seems to me like `golang.org/x/sys/windows.Signal` simply should not be used with the `os/signal` package, even though it implements the `os.Signal` interface (which seems misleading). This seems to be copied from the `syscall` package, which also uses the same scheme to invent Unix signals for Windows.

So from this perspective, I guess `os/signal` really is just meant to work only with `os.Interrupt` on Windows, which isn't stated explicitly by https://pkg.go.dev/os/signal#hdr-Windows as you point out.

We should definitely make the documentation more explicit.

issue.title: proposal: sync: new type `MapHash`

labels.name: LibraryProposal
labels.description: Issues describing a requested change to the Go standard library or x/ libraries, but not to a tool

milestone.title: Proposal
milestone.description:
Proposals that are pending (not yet accepted/rejected).
See https://go.dev/s/proposal-process.

issue.body:
# proposal: sync: new type `MapHash`

## Background

The sync package's `Map` type treats keys and values as type `any`, and requires that the keys are comparable. This both leaves performance on the table by frequently forcing simple value types to be boxed into an `any` and eschews type-safety, leading to lots of code that simply states the types of the keys and values in a comment, such as this (one of many) example from the standard library:

```go
var listCache sync.Map // map[string]listImports, keyed by contextName
```

Today we have generics, so we can make uses of `sync.Map` (which are the common ones) much more type-safe. The current situation is especially silly since the underlying implementation of a `Map` has actually been type-parameterized since Go 1.23!

One reason we haven't been able to move forward with a type-parameterized `sync.Map` in the `sync` package itself is that we had [no idea what to call it](https://github.com/golang/go/discussions/48287). [Creating a new `sync/v2` package](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/71076) was proposed as an alternative, but because only two types (`Pool` and `Map`) would actually benefit from generics at this time, we would be forced to incur all the costs of a `v2` package for just those types.

Also, @bradfitz has since [exported the internal implementation](https://github.com/go4org/hashtriemap) of `sync.Map`, `internal/sync.HashTrieMap`.

## Proposal

With the [standardization of the hash function](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/70471), there's an opportunity to modernize the `Map` type and give it a new, clear name: `MapHash`. The existing `Map` can be defined in terms of it.

I propose the following API changes for the `sync` package.

```go
type MapHash[K, V any, H maphash.Hasher[K]] struct{ ... }

func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) All() iter.Seq2[K, V]
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Clear()
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) CompareAndDelete(key K, old V) (deleted bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) CompareAndSwap(key K, old, new V) (swapped bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Delete(key K)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Load(key K) (value V, ok bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) LoadAndDelete(key K) (value V, loaded bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) LoadOrStore(key K, value V) (result V, loaded bool)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Range(yield func(key K, value V) bool) // for sync.Map
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Store(key K, old V)
func (*MapHash[K, V, H]) Swap(key K, new V) (previous V, loaded bool)

type Map = MapHash[any, any, maphash.ComparableHasher[any]]
```

This API is identical to `sync.Map` except that it makes use of the type parameters, and adds an idiomatic iterator with an `All` method that returns an `iter.Seq2`. This method has the same synchronization guarantees as `Range`.

**NOTE**: Do not pay too close attention to the type signature. It will change to follow whatever the broader consensus is on container type signatures. For now, this proposal simply matches #69559. The core part of the proposal is the addition of a new type with a custom `maphash.Hasher` in the existing sync package as a way to move forward on having at type-safe concurrent map. Changes to this part may affect some of the minor details in this proposal, such as whether we replace `Map` with a type alias.

### Rationale: Why a custom `maphash.Hasher`?

The use-case for a custom `maphash.Hasher` is admittedly niche. Most of the time a `comparable` key type is good enough. The downside of requiring a `maphash.Hasher` is that it complicates the API.

There are two reasons I propose supporting a custom hash function:
1. Consistency across the standard library.
2. The underlying implementation trivially supports it.

For (1), this API is intended to exist alongside https://github.com/golang/go/issues/69559, which proposes a map implementation with a custom hash function.

For (2), my concern is that eliding support for custom hash functions would just encourage [more unnecessary exports](https://github.com/go4org/hashtriemap) of the internal map implementation, precisely _because_ it can trivially support it. This makes the choice of concurrent map even more messy and complicated in the broader ecosystem. A good quality implementation already exists in the standard library.

### Alternative: Don't redefine `Map`

Alternatively, we don't define `Map` as a type alias, and instead just use the `MapHash` implementation under the hood. This way we don't need to add the `Range` method to `MapHash`.

One benefit of the type alias approach is that it may minimize the visibility of `Map` in the documentation when users go looking for a concurrent map.

### Optional: Shorthand for comparable keys

A downside of `MapHash` is that common cases are very verbose, requiring the developer to write out `maphash.ComparableHasher` everywhere the type is referenced. We can make this common case much less verbose with a generic type alias, too:

```go
type MapOf[K comparable, V any] = MapHash[K, V, maphash.ComparableHasher[K]]
```

The `MapOf` name is not intended to set a precedent or a new pattern that others should follow. It's a compromise that may or may not be a better alternative to creating a new `sync/v2` package.

## Implementation 

We would need to rework the underlying map implementation of `internal/sync.HashTrieMap` to use the new `maphash.Hasher`, but this is fairly trivial to do, since the equality and hash functions are already invoked indirectly. Then we would be able to refactor `internal/sync.HashTrieMap` into the implementation of `sync.MapHash` in the `sync` package and remove the `internal/sync` package, which would be a nice internal cleanup.

## Go 1 Compatibility

This proposal only adds a new API, so it's backward compatible. Although `sync.Map` is redefined, it still exposes an identical API to today, with the sole addition of the `All` method.

## Alternatives

As mentioned earlier, we could choose to pursue a `sync/v2` package wholesale instead, for which [a proposal](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/71076) already exists.

I don't feel strongly about either, but at this point the `sync/v2` proposal has fallen behind a little bit (namely around the addition of `maphash.Hasher`), though it could be trivially updated to accommodate it.

## Acknowledgements

Thank you to Cherry, David, and Russ for your initial feedback on this proposal.

comment.body:
> Given this, why isn’t HashTrieMap exposed as a public?

Because it never went through the proposal process. I am the author of `HashTrieMap`. Exposing it is what I'm trying to do with this proposal. Am I missing something?

> Perhaps this is something a future sync/v2 could address.

That's addressed in the proposal text, and mentioned in the discussion above.

> Regarding implementing custom hasher, we could do that via custom stringer:

There's already an approved proposal for custom hashers. Why would we use strings?

comment.body:
CC @golang/windows 

Signal handling on Windows looks pretty different than on Unix platforms, so I'm not surprised a bogus signal results in something bad happening. Better documentation seems like a good first step.

Looking at x/sys/windows, it seems we already "invent" some bogus values that correspond to Unix signals, so I guess this just gets reinterpreted at some lower layer. (https://cs.opensource.google/go/x/sys/+/master:windows/types_windows.go;l=33;drc=ea436ef09d96eabb81a7a9aa95e7b57c793c6855) I'll have to dig into it more though.

issue.body:
### Background

A `mallocgc` caller can set the `needzero` parameter to false to say that the caller is taking responsibility for overwriting any bytes that will be visible to the user, and hence stating that the `mallocgc` code does not need to zero out the allocated bytes that will be overwritten before ever being visible to normal user code. This is only permitted with noscan allocations (heap objects that do not contain pointers).

```
func mallocgc(size uintptr, typ *_type, needzero bool) unsafe.Pointer
```

The common case for `mallocgc` is `needzero` is set to true. In that case, `mallocgc` and the related functions are careful to issue a publication barrier after the memory is zeroed, which helps ensure user programs do not observe uninitialized memory in the presence of races including on weakly ordered machines. The publication barrier makes it so that the zeroing stores cannot be reordered with the store that publishes the allocated pointer.

### Problem

However, while reviewing the `runtime.freegc` work in #74299, @RLH noticed a general long-standing problem that most or perhaps all current callers of `mallocgc` with needzero=false do not seem to be issuing publication barriers.

In https://github.com/golang/go/issues/74299#issuecomment-3553032552, @RLH wrote:

> I'm not sure the GC can relinquish "do not zero" responsibility to the caller given the Go memory model outlawing out of thin air or acausal writes. Before Go got its memory model act together out of thin air values were fine in racy programs. I looked at some of the slice code and it does not mention out of thin air hazards or seem to have any publication barriers logic so I suspect the code does not deal with the issue. Furthermore they seem to not use the optimization for pointer values so they seem to working around this bug when pointers are used but not the general problem. Feel free to correct me, memory model stuff in racy programs is complicated.

and @RLH continued in https://github.com/golang/go/issues/74299#issuecomment-3572703849:

> The larger issue is that anyone calling the allocator with a needszero == false gets out of thin air values back and must issue a publicationBarrier once all values have been initialized but before the library routine publishes. Perhaps my code grep abilities have gotten rusty but I do not see any calls to publicationBarrier in the go repository code after calls to mallocgc with needzero == false. We can fix it by adding publicationBarriers to the Go library code.

I believe @mknyszek concurred this is a general problem in https://github.com/golang/go/issues/74299#issuecomment-3572770335:

> I think you're correct that these are missing in general. Of course, we won't notice on x86...

----

I have the start of a CL that I plan to send to add the missing publication barriers (in a few places in the runtime, and also handle elsewhere like the `strings` and `bytes` packages via `internal/bytealg.MakeNoZero`, and possibly elsewhere).

That said, this is a subtle area, so I'd be of course fine if the core Go team prefers to do this themselves.

issue.title: runtime, cmd/compile: add runtime.freegc and runtime.freegcTracked to reduce GC work (GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc)

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: Implementation
labels.description: Issues describing a semantics-preserving change to the Go implementation.

issue.body:
**UPDATE** (Nov 27, 2025): 

* The design doc is now published in the Go proposal repo: https://go.dev/design/74299-runtime-freegc
* Merged CLs:
   * [runtime: add GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefree](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/700235)
   * [runtime: set GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc to disabled by default](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/720120)
   * [runtime: add runtime.freegc to reduce GC work](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/673695)
   * [runtime: add more precise test of assist credit handling for runtime.freegc](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/717520)
   * [runtime: support runtime.freegc in size-specialized mallocs for noscan objects](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/715761)
   * [cmd/compile: introduce alias analysis and automatically free non-aliased memory after growslice](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/710015)

Additional CLs are in progress, including some additional compiler-focused ones.   
   
The best place to start is probably the "Introduction" section of the [design doc](https://go.dev/design/74299-runtime-freegc). There are also some sample performance results in that document.

The remainder of this comment below here is from ~June, 2025, and is probably less interesting at this point than the design doc.

----

Opening this issue here to help with discussion.

### Background

[CL 673695](https://go.dev/cl/673695) starts with:

> For reducing allocations, there is sometimes a chicken and egg problem.
> 
> For example, #72036 is a WIP approach to update escape analysis so that interface arguments do not always escape, with a primary target of w.Write(buf) and friends, but many interesting byte slices are variable size known only at runtime. Currently, a make with an unknown capacity (beyond 32 bytes) causes a slice to be heap allocated, so solely improving escape analysis in this case has limited impact on actual applications -- if a slice is heap allocated for two reasons, eliminating one reason just means it is still heap allocated for the other reason, which lessens the value of doing the work to eliminate the first reason.
> 
> In an attempt to help triangulate on what is possible for the runtime, compiler, and standard library to do together to reduce how much work the GC must do, this CL has a proof-of-concept set of runtime.free implementations

Back in February, as part of my write up for #72036 I mentioned that a runtime.free could help with this chicken and egg problem:

> I have a mostly working runtime.free that can be inserted by the compiler (and not for use by normal Go code of course), and I'm looking at hooking it up a few different ways, including when a slice is both variable size and also used as an interface method arg. (I'll probably open a separate issue on this, but I'm also attempting to free memory in some runtime map growth cases, some append cases, etc.

### Prototype

[CL 673695](https://go.dev/cl/673695) contains that mostly working runtime.free. (It's "mostly working" in the sense that it can pass its own tests, including I let it accumulate a few hundred CPU hours running its tests successfully under `stress`. That of course does not mean it is bug free, and it still has some shortcuts, including for example how it updates user-facing stats).

I also have some additional WIP CLs that update the compiler to automatically insert free calls for slices allocated via `make` when the compiler can prove it is safe to do so, and separately updating maps to free map storage during growth and split as well as a small number of lower-level stdlib things like `strings.Builder` and `bytes.Buffer` (which are "low level" in the sense that they already encapsulate some low-level performance tricks, including use of unsafe, but also they let other higher-level pieces of the stdlib then safely benefit from their performance). It is not intended that higher-level portions of the stdlib (like net/http) would have manual calls to a runtime.free.

Patterns of `append` usage like `slices.Collect` and `slices.AppendSeq` are also targets, with some good initial results. (`slices.Collect` for example could be updated manually to start, which I've tried as an experiment, or it could wait for the compiler to automatically recognize its pattern).

Note that runtime.free and the related runtime entry points are not public APIs, and cannot be called directly by end users.

### Basic approach

See the more detailed write-up in the [CL 673695](https://go.dev/cl/673695) commit message, which covers these 4 runtime entry points to start:
```
 func free(ptr unsafe.Pointer)
 func freesized(ptr unsafe.Pointer, uintptr size, noscan bool)
 func freetracked(trackedObjs *[]trackedObj)
 func makeslicetracked64(et *_type, len64 int64, cap64 int64, trackedObjs *[]trackedObj) unsafe.Pointer
```

At least for the approach I have so far, the free work tries to take advantage of things the compiler already knows or that the compiler is taught with modest effort (for example in escape analysis).

### Performance

Some preliminary benchmarks. See [CL 673695](https://go.dev/cl/673695) for commentary on these benchmarks.

#### strings.Builder.Write with runtime.freesized

```
                                               │       old        │    new                              │
                                               │      sec/op      │   sec/op     vs base                │
BuildString_Builder/1Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           55.82n ± 2%   55.86n ± 2%        ~ (p=0.794 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/2Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           125.2n ± 2%   115.4n ± 1%   -7.86% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/3Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           224.0n ± 1%   188.2n ± 2%  -16.00% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/5Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           239.1n ± 9%   205.1n ± 1%  -14.20% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/8Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4           422.8n ± 3%   325.4n ± 1%  -23.04% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/10Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4          436.9n ± 2%   342.3n ± 1%  -21.64% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/100Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4         4.403µ ± 1%   2.381µ ± 2%  -45.91% (p=0.000 n=20)
BuildString_Builder/1000Write_36Bytes_NoGrow-4        48.28µ ± 2%   21.38µ ± 2%  -55.71% (p=0.000 n=20)
```

(In short, currently `strings.Builder` incrementally builds up an internal buffer, growing as needed. The modification introduced N-1 frees for N allocations, with the final allocation going to the user. There is only 1 allocation if there is only 1 `Builder.Write` call, so there is no benefit seen on the first line, but the benefit grows to ~2x faster as there are more allocations per benchmark loop).

#### Normal allocation (with these changes, but no free/reuse happening)

The new reuse code path for reusing a freed object is currently somewhat interwoven with the normal allocation path in runtime/malloc.go, but some initial micro-benchmarks suggest the impact of these changes on the normal allocation path when no reuse is happening could be small to negligible:


```
                        │         old        │  new                             │
                        │        sec/op      │ sec/op     vs base               │
Malloc8-16                      11.01n ± 1%   10.94n ± 1%  -0.68% (p=0.038 n=20)
Malloc16-16                     17.15n ± 1%   17.05n ± 0%  -0.55% (p=0.007 n=20)
Malloc32-16                     18.65n ± 1%   18.42n ± 0%  -1.26% (p=0.000 n=20)
MallocTypeInfo8-16              18.63n ± 0%   18.36n ± 0%  -1.45% (p=0.000 n=20)
MallocTypeInfo16-16             22.32n ± 0%   22.65n ± 0%  +1.50% (p=0.000 n=20)
MallocTypeInfo32-16             23.37n ± 0%   23.89n ± 0%  +2.23% (p=0.000 n=20)
geomean                         18.02n        18.01n       -0.05%
```

The initial performance seems promising, but still preliminary.

In general, some possible performance benefits might include:

 1. less CPU used by GC, which might matter most when CPU is constrained or exhausted (either on a sustained basis, or over some shorter but meaningful time interval).
 2. longer time between GCs, with less time overall with the write barrier enabled (helping application code execute faster).
 3. more cache-friendly allocs for user code (esp. if it ends up with LIFO or near-LIFO free/alloc).
 4. with less GC work overall, maybe fewer assists or other ways the GC can intrude on the app.
 5. maybe slightly more friendly to the new Green Tea GC if it can result in a higher density per span (speculation 😅).

The first maybe is the most obvious, but that might not justify the change for apps that have spare CPU and the GC CPU costs are almost entirely in the "background" compared to the rest of the app work.

The third is particularly interesting because it might help make it more of a win in CPU costs even before considering the costs of running the GC. (I suspect I've observed this effect in some of the benchmarks, but need to look more carefully).

One approach to mitigate the cost for applications that don't care much about GC costs today is the free-related work currently has a size-based threshold (currently 16 bytes). That could be raised to something higher than 16 to the point where the other costs of allocating (including usually zeroing) or operating on the result (like writing data and doing useful work) are large enough costs compared to the overhead of the free-related work such that the free-related overhead is small enough. (For compiler-inserted frees of slices, if the threshold does need to increase from 16, it would be nice if the free threshold could be low enough to match the 32-byte threshold for new Go 1.25 stack allocation optimization introduced by Keith, or maybe that 32-byte threshold ends up being higher). Also, the current cost in the prototype likely can be made cheaper.

Another approach to manage the costs could be via PGO, though I'm hopeful a first version could be viable without requiring PGO.

That said, it likely might be easier to sort through some of the performance trade-offs by seeing more concrete performance examples, which is one of the main goals of this current prototype.

---------

There are multiple ways that runtime.free and related entry points could be used, but I'm hoping this issue could be focused on some possible first steps.

In any event, this is still exploratory. @mknyszek took a look at the code a ~month ago and gave some helpful initial feedback. Absent someone pointing out a fatal flaw in the overall approach, I plan to try to polish things up some more and get more feedback. It might be that this exact approach does not pan out, but maybe we'll learn something via the exploration.

comment.body: It's a `GOEXPERIMENT` in Go 1.26, yes, but that's mostly just for continued development. I wouldn't expect a huge impact from it yet, and I would hesitate to use it in production (it needs a dedicated builder, and more testing).

issue.title: internal/trace: recorder.Write returns 0 instead of n when buffer contains only header

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
### Go version

1.25

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
AR='ar'
CC='gcc'
CGO_CFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_CPPFLAGS=''
CGO_CXXFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_ENABLED='0'
CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_LDFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CXX='g++'
GCCGO='gccgo'
GO111MODULE=''
GOAMD64='v1'
GOARCH='amd64'
GOAUTH='netrc'
GOBIN=''
GOCACHE='/home/jpinto/.cache/go-build'
GOCACHEPROG=''
GODEBUG=''
GOENV='/home/jpinto/.config/go/env'
GOEXE=''
GOEXPERIMENT=''
GOFIPS140='off'
GOFLAGS=''
GOGCCFLAGS='-fPIC -m64 -fno-caret-diagnostics -Qunused-arguments -Wl,--no-gc-sections -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=/tmp/go-build3972747715=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches'
GOHOSTARCH='amd64'
GOHOSTOS='linux'
GOINSECURE=''
GOMOD='/dev/null'
GOMODCACHE='/home/jpinto/go/pkg/mod'
GONOPROXY=''
GONOSUMDB=''
GOOS='linux'
GOPATH='/home/jpinto/go'
GOPRIVATE=''
GOPROXY='https://proxy.golang.org,direct'
GOROOT='/usr/local/go'
GOSUMDB='sum.golang.org'
GOTELEMETRY='local'
GOTELEMETRYDIR='/home/jpinto/.config/go/telemetry'
GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='auto'
GOTOOLDIR='/usr/local/go/pkg/tool/linux_amd64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.25.1'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

I was reviewing the code in `src/internal/trace/recorder.go` and noticed 
that the `Write` method returns `0, nil` when it successfully 
processes a 16-byte header, even though it consumed all 16 bytes from the 
input buffer.

### What did you see happen?

In `src/internal/trace/recorder.go`, the `Write` method returns `0, nil` 
when the input buffer contains exactly 16 bytes (just the header), even 
though it successfully consumed all 16 bytes.


### What did you expect to see?

The method should return `16, nil` to correctly implement the `io.Writer` 
interface contract, which requires returning the number of bytes consumed.


### What did you do?

I was reviewing the code in `src/internal/trace/recorder.go` and noticed 
that the `Write` method returns `0, nil` when it successfully 
processes a 16-byte header, even though it consumed all 16 bytes from the 
input buffer.

### What did you see happen?

In `src/internal/trace/recorder.go`, the `Write` method returns `0, nil` 
when the input buffer contains exactly 16 bytes (just the header), even 
though it successfully consumed all 16 bytes.


### What did you expect to see?

The method should return `16, nil` to correctly implement the `io.Writer` 
interface contract, which requires returning the number of bytes consumed.

comment.body: Thanks for the report. If you're up for sending a fix, feel free to do so (https://go.dev/doc/contribute), otherwise I'll get to it soon.

summary_topics.discuss: This user engages deeply with the Go programming language's core infrastructure, focusing on runtime stability, compiler optimizations, and build system reliability. Key problems include goroutine leaks, memory management issues like span corruption, platform-specific bugs (especially Windows and ARM64), and test failures in critical components like cmd/link and runtime. Technologies involved are Go itself, Cgo, system calls, and distributed build systems like LUCI.

+            simple = self.get_package_simple(package_name)
+            meta_original = self.merge_simple_info_into_metadata(meta_original, simple)
+        except PackageNotFoundError:
+            # Some mirrors may not implement PEP 691 simple API, just go ahead
comment.body: 另外 PyPI 拿到的 meta_original 里面就是有 core-metadata，不需要再自己 merge 吧？只有在上游是没有实现 PEP 658/714 的 shadowmire 同步的 pypi repo 的时候才有必要。

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -1091,6 +1155,12 @@ def do_update(
             self.local_db.set(package_name, -1)
             return None
 
+        try:
+            simple = self.get_package_simple(package_name)
+            meta_original = self.merge_simple_info_into_metadata(meta_original, simple)
+        except PackageNotFoundError:
+            # Some mirrors may not implement PEP 691 simple API, just go ahead
comment.body: PyPI 不太可能没有实现这个？

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -426,6 +444,17 @@ def gen_html_file_tags(release: dict) -> str:
                 else:
                     file_tags += ' data-yanked=""'
 
+            # data-metadata: digest_name (sha256)
+            if "core-metadata" in release and release["core-metadata"]:
+                metadata = release["core-metadata"]
+                if cls.digest_name in metadata and metadata[cls.digest_name]:
+                    file_tags += (
+                        f' data-dist-info-metadata="{cls.digest_name}={html.escape(metadata[cls.digest_name])}"'
comment.body: 根据 PEP 714 的话（<https://peps.python.org/pep-0714/>），是不是可以直接去掉 data-dist-info-metadata 的支持？考虑到这个 PEP 是 2023 年的事情了。

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -362,6 +362,24 @@ def get_package_metadata(self, package_name: str) -> dict:
             raise PackageNotFoundError
         return req.json()  # type: ignore
 
+    def get_package_simple(self, package_name: str) -> dict:
+        # Based on PEP 691
+        headers = {"Accept": "application/vnd.pypi.simple.v1+json"}
+        req = self.session.get(
+            urljoin(self.host, f"simple/{package_name}/"), headers=headers
+        )
+        # For incorrectly configured shadowmire mirrors that do not return correct content-type
comment.body: `PyPI` class 不应该考虑用 shadowmire 同步的 upstream——这是 `SyncPlainHTTP` 要做的事情。而且如果放这里的话，对 "incorrectly configured mirrors" 那么同步的请求数就直接乘二了，最好不要这么做。

issue.title: 使用 LABEL 标注同步容器与 bindIP 配置的兼容性
issue.body:
Yuki 的 `bindIP` 依赖于同步容器主动实现 `bind()` 支持，但是对于某些程序很困难（需要 LD_PRELOAD 一类的 trick）或者根本不可行，因此目前 Yuki 也提供 Docker Network 支持。对于没有实现处理 `BIND_ADDRESS` 环境变量的同步容器，如果能在加载配置的时候显示 warning 可以帮助管理员快速发现配置问题，而不是以为配置生效，但是实际上没有。

同步容器的 Dockerfile 可能可以增加和下面类似的东西：

```dockerfile
# LABEL bind_support=true
LABEL bind_support=false
```

issue.title: Deprecate bindIP
issue.body:
在 https://201.ustclug.org/ops/virtualization/container/#docker-bridge 写了使用 Docker network 的配置方法。并且已知的其他用户也没有大量使用 `bindIP` 的，因此目前来看可以逐步淘汰掉 bindIP 配置：

- 现有的同步容器仍然支持 `BIND_ADDRESS` 环境变量
- 新的同步容器默认不再支持 `BIND_ADDRESS`
- 下一个 Yuki 版本开始对配置的 `bindIP` 输出警告，并在未来的某个版本删除支持

issue.title: [CI/Build][Docker] Add centralized version manifest for Docker builds

labels.name: ready
labels.description: ONLY add when PR is ready to merge/full CI is needed

issue.body:
## Purpose

Introduces `docker/versions.json` as a centralized, machine-readable version manifest for all pinned dependencies in Docker builds.

## Problem

Version information is currently scattered across `docker/Dockerfile` and various install scripts, making it hard to:
- Track version changes across releases
- Programmatically query versions for CI/tooling
- Maintain consistency

## Solution

**Dockerfile = Source of Truth** with auto-generated bake-native `versions.json`.

## Integration with Docker Bake (#31477)

This PR complements the `docker buildx bake` work in #31477:

| This PR | Bake PR #31477 |
|---------|----------------|
| `versions.json` = version data | `docker-bake.hcl` = build config |
| What versions to use | How to build |

### Bake-Native JSON Format

```json
{
  "variable": {
    "CUDA_VERSION": { "default": "12.9.1" },
    "PYTHON_VERSION": { "default": "3.12" },
    "torch_cuda_arch_list": { "default": "7.0 7.5 8.0 8.9 9.0 10.0 12.0" },
    "FLASHINFER_VERSION": { "default": "0.5.3" },
    "DEEPGEMM_GIT_REF": { "default": "594953acce41793ae00a1233eb516044d604bcb6" },
    ...
  }
}
```

Bake reads this directly - no preprocessing needed:
```bash
docker buildx bake -f docker/docker-bake.hcl -f docker/versions.json
```

### Auto-Generation

`tools/generate_versions_json.py` parses Dockerfile ARG defaults and generates bake-native versions.json:

```bash
python tools/generate_versions_json.py        # Generate
python tools/generate_versions_json.py --check  # CI validation
```

## Usage

```bash
# Simple docker build (uses Dockerfile defaults)
docker build -f docker/Dockerfile .

# Bake with versions.json
docker buildx bake -f docker/docker-bake.hcl -f docker/versions.json

# Override specific versions
docker buildx bake --set "*.args.CUDA_VERSION=12.8"

# Extract versions programmatically (e.g., for downstream packaging)
jq -r '.variable.CUDA_VERSION.default' docker/versions.json
```

## Benefits

1. **Single Source of Truth**: Dockerfile ARG defaults are authoritative
2. **Bake-Native**: versions.json works directly with `docker buildx bake`
3. **Machine-Readable**: Easy to parse with `jq` for CI and external tooling  
4. **Extension Commits Centralized**: DEEPGEMM_GIT_REF, PPLX_COMMIT_HASH, DEEPEP_COMMIT_HASH moved to Dockerfile
5. **Cleaner Diffs**: Version bumps show as clear changes
6. **Low Barrier**: Contributors can still use `docker build .` without learning bake

## Files Changed

- `docker/versions.json` - Bake-native version manifest (auto-generated)
- `docker/Dockerfile` - Source of truth for version defaults
- `tools/generate_versions_json.py` - Auto-generation script with --check mode

## Test Plan

- [x] JSON is valid and parseable by bake
- [x] `docker buildx bake --print` shows correct variable values
- [x] `python tools/generate_versions_json.py --check` passes
- [x] Pre-commit checks pass

comment.body: Hi @mritunjaysharma394, thanks! Do you think it is necessary and appropriate to switch some steps in the release pipeline to also use `bake` and this new version manifest?

issue.title: [CI/Build] Fix ffmpeg CVEs
issue.body:
## Purpose

The current vLLM Docker images contain **28 security vulnerabilities** in FFmpeg and related libraries, including **3 Critical** and **11 High** severity CVEs. These vulnerabilities stem from using Ubuntu's universe repository version of FFmpeg (4.4.2), which requires Ubuntu Pro subscription for security patches.

The vulnerabilities exist because:

1. **Ubuntu Universe Repository**: vLLM uses FFmpeg from Ubuntu's universe repository
2. **No Free Security Updates**: Ubuntu universe packages require **Ubuntu Pro subscription** for security patches
3. **Outdated Version**: FFmpeg 4.4.2 has known vulnerabilities that remain unpatched in the free Ubuntu repositories

### Affected Files:
- Any Dockerfiles in the repository that install FFmpeg from Ubuntu

### Impact

#### Vulnerability Breakdown
- **Critical Severity**: 3 CVEs
- **High Severity**: 11 CVEs  
- **Medium Severity**: 14 CVEs
- **Total**: 28 unique CVEs

#### Affected Components
All FFmpeg-related libraries in vLLM Docker images (version 4.4.2):
- `ffmpeg`
- `libavcodec58`, `libavdevice58`, `libavfilter7`, `libavformat58`
- `libavresample4`, `libavutil56`, `libpostproc55`
- `libswresample3`, `libswscale5`

### Critical CVEs (Immediate Action Required)

| CVE ID | Severity | Component | Reference |
|--------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| CVE-2024-35366 | **CRITICAL** | ffmpeg 4.4.2 | [Ubuntu Security](https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35366.html) |
| CVE-2024-35367 | **CRITICAL** | ffmpeg 4.4.2 | [Ubuntu Security](https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35367.html) |
| CVE-2024-35368 | **CRITICAL** | ffmpeg 4.4.2 | [Ubuntu Security](https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35368.html) |

### CVE Summary

<details>
<summary>Click to expand CVE Summary </summary>

## CVE Summary

| CVE ID | Severity | Reference URL |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| CVE-2024-35368 | CRITICAL | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35368.html |
| CVE-2024-35366 | CRITICAL | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35366.html |

### Solution:
Replace Ubuntu's vulnerable FFmpeg 4.4.2 with security-patched Debian Bullseye version 4.3.9-0+deb11u1 to eliminate all 28 CVEs without requiring Ubuntu Pro subscription.

## Test Plan
CI Test
- https://buildkite.com/vllm/ci/builds/47260

## Test Result
Pass, failed `v1-test-attention-b200` also fail on main.

</details>

---

> [!NOTE]
> Pins secure Debian Bullseye `ffmpeg` and related libraries in the `vllm-base` Docker stage to mitigate vulnerabilities.
> 
> - Adds `gnupg`, configures Debian Bullseye repos and keyrings, and installs pinned `ffmpeg=7:4.3.9-0+deb11u1` with associated `libav*` and `libchromaprint1`
> - Removes prior unpinned Ubuntu `ffmpeg` install; cleans up Bullseye sources/keyrings post-install to keep image lean
> - No other build stages or application code changed
>

> - Remove prior unpinned `ffmpeg` install; delete temporary Bullseye sources and keys after install to keep image clean

comment.body: Great work! I think caching should be considered to make the nightly tests practical. The fresh build time of vllm and all dependencies can easily go over 6 hours on our build agents, so we can hardly afford a fresh rebuild for any PR updates. For example, #30908 would very much need the nightly tests.

 #
 # designrulecheck/Netlist.java
 #
-BuildingNetlistFor =正在为工作表“%s”生成网表
-CircuitInfoString =电路“%s”有 %d 个网络和 %d 条总线。
-DRCPassesString =电路“%s”通过 DRC 检查。
-EmptyNamedSheet =在您的设计中找到一个名称为空的工作表。这是不允许的，请指定名称！
-FoundBadComponent =发现电路“%2$s”中的组件“%1$s”
-HDL_CompNameIsLabel =找到标签等于电路名称的一个或多个组件。这不受支持。
-HDL_DuplicatedLabels =找到一个或多个重复的标签。请使标签名称唯一。
-HDL_LabelInvalid =发现无效的标签。
-HDL_noLabel =找到一个或多个没有标签的组件。请给它们贴上标签或使用批注功能。
-HDL_Tristate =找到一个或多个组件的三态驱动程序或浮动输出。这不受支持。
-HDL_unsupported =在电路中发现一个或多个不支持 HDL 生成的组件。
-MultipleSheetSameName =在您的设计中找到多张名称为“%s”的工作表。这是不允许的，请确保所有工作表都有唯一的名称！
-NetAdd_ComponentWidthMismatch =发现连接在一起的两个组件具有不同的位宽。
-NetList_BitwidthError =发现位宽连接问题。
-NetList_CircuitGated =->门控实例
-NetList_CircuitGatedNotGated =找到与选通和非选通时钟组件一起使用的电路（请参阅下面的跟踪列表）。这只适用于支持的最简单的情况！
-NetList_CircuitNotGated =->非门控实例
-NetList_duplicatedSplitter =在同一位置发现相同的分离器。
-NetList_emptynets =发现未连接的网络！
-NetList_GatedClock =找到了一个门控时钟。请小心，真正的硬件可能无法正常工作！
-NetList_GatedClockInt =->中间选通时钟线
-NetList_GatedClockSink =->选通时钟线的接收器
-NetList_GatedClockSource =->门控时钟线源
-NetList_IOError =找到一个或多个具有 I/O 针脚的组件，这不受支持。
-NetList_NoClockConnection =找到时钟输入未连接的组件！
-NetList_NoEndSplitterConnections =找到带有一个或多个针脚的分离器，这些针脚连接到网络，但没有连接到根总线。
-NetList_NoSplitterConnection =发现不能将信号从总线传输到扇出的分离器。
-NetList_NoSplitterEndConnections =发现具有一个或多个未连接的扇出针脚的分离器。
-NetList_PossibleGatedClock =在顶层发现可能的门控时钟！请小心，真正的硬件可能无法正常工作！
-NetList_ShortCircuit =找到具有多个驱动器的网络（短路）。
-NetList_SourceWithoutSink =找到没有（A）个接收器的源！
-# ==> NetList_TraceListBegin =
-# ==> NetList_TraceListEnd =
-NetList_UnconnectedInput =发现未连接的输入（位）！
-NetList_UnconnectedInputs =找到输入未连接的组件！
-NetList_UnconnectedOutput =发现未连接的输出（位）！
-NetList_UnsourcedSink =发现了一个无源的水槽！
-NetListBuild =正在为电路“%s”（%d/7）构建网表
-NetMerge_BitWidthError =正在尝试合并不同大小的网络。
-TopLevelNoIO =顶层“%s”没有输入和/或输出！
+BuildingNetlistFor = 正在为页面“%s”构建网表
+CircuitInfoString = 电路“%s”包含 %d 条网络和 %d 条总线
+DRCPassesString = 电路“%s”通过 DRC 检查
+EmptyNamedSheet = 页面名称不得为空，请指定名称
+FoundBadComponent = 在电路“%s”中发现元件“%s”
+HDL_CompNameIsLabel = 元件标签不得与电路名称相同
+HDL_DuplicatedLabels = 发现重复标签，请确保标签名称唯一
+HDL_LabelInvalid = 发现无效标签
+HDL_noLabel = 存在未设置标签的元件，请为其设置标签或使用自动标注功能
+HDL_Tristate = 不支持三态驱动或悬空输出
+HDL_unsupported = 电路中存在不支持生成 HDL 的元件
+MultipleSheetSameName = 页面名称“%s”重复，请确保所有页面名称唯一
+NetAdd_ComponentWidthMismatch = 相连元件位宽不一致
+NetList_BitwidthError = 发现位宽连接问题
+NetList_CircuitGated = ----> 门控实例
+NetList_CircuitGatedNotGated = 同一电路同时用于门控时钟与非门控时钟，仅支持最简单情况，详见下方追踪列表
+NetList_CircuitNotGated = ----> 非门控实例
+NetList_duplicatedSplitter = 同一位置存在重复分线器
+NetList_emptynets = 存在未连接的网络
+NetList_GatedClock = 发现门控时钟，请注意实际硬件可能无法正常工作
+NetList_GatedClockInt = ----> 中间门控时钟线
+NetList_GatedClockSink = ----> 门控时钟线的接收端
+NetList_GatedClockSource = ----> 门控时钟线的源端
+NetList_IOError = 不支持带 I/O 引脚的元件
+NetList_NoClockConnection = 存在未连接时钟输入的元件
+NetList_NoEndSplitterConnections = 分线器的某些引脚连接到网络，但未连接到根总线
+NetList_NoSplitterConnection = 分线器未将信号从总线传输到分支
+NetList_NoSplitterEndConnections = 分线器存在未连接的分支引脚
+NetList_PossibleGatedClock = 顶层可能存在门控时钟，请注意实际硬件可能无法正常工作
+NetList_ShortCircuit = 同一网络存在多个驱动源（短路）
+NetList_SourceWithoutSink = 存在没有接收端的源
+NetList_TraceListBegin = ===> 追踪列表开始
+NetList_TraceListEnd = ===> 追踪列表结束
+NetList_UnconnectedInput = 存在未连接的输入位
+NetList_UnconnectedInputs = 元件存在未连接的输入
+NetList_UnconnectedOutput = 存在未连接的输出位
+NetList_UnsourcedSink = 存在没有源的接收端
+NetListBuild = 正在为电路“%s”构建网表（%d/7）
+NetMerge_BitWidthError = 尝试合并不同位宽的网络
+TopLevelNoIO = 顶层电路“%s”缺少输入和/或输出

 #
 # download/Download.java
 #
-FPGABoardNotConnected =找不到连接的 FPGA 板。
-FPGABoardSelection =选择要编程的电路板
-FPGACancelWait =正在取消，请稍候…
-FPGADownloadAborted =下载已中止。
-FPGADownloadBitfile =将设计下载到电路板上。
-FPGADownloadCancel =否，中止
-FpgaDownloadInfo =生成 FPGA 文件并执行下载；这可能需要一段时间
-FPGADownloadOk =是，下载
-FPGAExecutionFailure =在阶段“%s”中发现错误
-FpgaGuiCanceling =取消！
-FPGAInterrupted =被用户中断。
-FPGAInterruptedError =%s 下载中断！
-FPGAIOError =%s 下载中出现内部 IO 异常
-FPGAMapNotComplete =并非所有 IO 组件都已映射到板“%s”；请映射所有组件以继续！
-FPGAMultipleBoards =我找到%d 块附着板。选择要编程的项目.
-FPGANameContainsSpaces =文件“%s”包含空格。\nHDL 合成引擎不允许使用空格。\n请重命名您的文件和目录，使其不包含任何空格。
-FPGAState0 =执行设计规则检查（DRC）
-FPGAState1 =生成硬件描述语言（HDL）文件

-FPGAState3 =将 I/O 资源映射到板上
-FPGAState4 =创建下载脚本
-FPGAStaticExecutionFailure =执行时出错。
-FPGAVerifyMsg1 =确认您的主板已连接，并且您已准备好下载。
-FPGAVerifyMsg2 =准备好下载了吗？

 #

 #
-BuildingNetlistFor =正在为工作表“%s”生成网表
-CircuitInfoString =电路“%s”有 %d 个网络和 %d 条总线。
-DRCPassesString =电路“%s”通过 DRC 检查。
-EmptyNamedSheet =在您的设计中找到一个名称为空的工作表。这是不允许的，请指定名称！
-FoundBadComponent =发现电路“%2$s”中的组件“%1$s”
-HDL_CompNameIsLabel =找到标签等于电路名称的一个或多个组件。这不受支持。
-HDL_DuplicatedLabels =找到一个或多个重复的标签。请使标签名称唯一。
-HDL_LabelInvalid =发现无效的标签。
-HDL_noLabel =找到一个或多个没有标签的组件。请给它们贴上标签或使用批注功能。
-HDL_Tristate =找到一个或多个组件的三态驱动程序或浮动输出。这不受支持。
-HDL_unsupported =在电路中发现一个或多个不支持 HDL 生成的组件。
-MultipleSheetSameName =在您的设计中找到多张名称为“%s”的工作表。这是不允许的，请确保所有工作表都有唯一的名称！
-NetAdd_ComponentWidthMismatch =发现连接在一起的两个组件具有不同的位宽。
-NetList_BitwidthError =发现位宽连接问题。
-NetList_CircuitGated =->门控实例
-NetList_CircuitGatedNotGated =找到与选通和非选通时钟组件一起使用的电路（请参阅下面的跟踪列表）。这只适用于支持的最简单的情况！
-NetList_CircuitNotGated =->非门控实例
-NetList_duplicatedSplitter =在同一位置发现相同的分离器。
-NetList_emptynets =发现未连接的网络！
-NetList_GatedClock =找到了一个门控时钟。请小心，真正的硬件可能无法正常工作！
-NetList_GatedClockInt =->中间选通时钟线
-NetList_GatedClockSink =->选通时钟线的接收器
-NetList_GatedClockSource =->门控时钟线源
-NetList_IOError =找到一个或多个具有 I/O 针脚的组件，这不受支持。
-NetList_NoClockConnection =找到时钟输入未连接的组件！
-NetList_NoEndSplitterConnections =找到带有一个或多个针脚的分离器，这些针脚连接到网络，但没有连接到根总线。
-NetList_NoSplitterConnection =发现不能将信号从总线传输到扇出的分离器。
-NetList_NoSplitterEndConnections =发现具有一个或多个未连接的扇出针脚的分离器。
-NetList_PossibleGatedClock =在顶层发现可能的门控时钟！请小心，真正的硬件可能无法正常工作！
-NetList_ShortCircuit =找到具有多个驱动器的网络（短路）。
-NetList_SourceWithoutSink =找到没有（A）个接收器的源！
-# ==> NetList_TraceListBegin =
-# ==> NetList_TraceListEnd =
-NetList_UnconnectedInput =发现未连接的输入（位）！
-NetList_UnconnectedInputs =找到输入未连接的组件！
-NetList_UnconnectedOutput =发现未连接的输出（位）！
-NetList_UnsourcedSink =发现了一个无源的水槽！
-NetListBuild =正在为电路“%s”（%d/7）构建网表
-NetMerge_BitWidthError =正在尝试合并不同大小的网络。
-TopLevelNoIO =顶层“%s”没有输入和/或输出！
+BuildingNetlistFor = 正在为页面“%s”构建网表
+CircuitInfoString = 电路“%s”包含 %d 条网络和 %d 条总线
+DRCPassesString = 电路“%s”通过 DRC 检查
+EmptyNamedSheet = 页面名称不得为空，请指定名称
+FoundBadComponent = 在电路“%s”中发现元件“%s”
+HDL_CompNameIsLabel = 元件标签不得与电路名称相同
+HDL_DuplicatedLabels = 发现重复标签，请确保标签名称唯一
+HDL_LabelInvalid = 发现无效标签
+HDL_noLabel = 存在未设置标签的元件，请为其设置标签或使用自动标注功能
+HDL_Tristate = 不支持三态驱动或悬空输出
+HDL_unsupported = 电路中存在不支持生成 HDL 的元件
+MultipleSheetSameName = 页面名称“%s”重复，请确保所有页面名称唯一
+NetAdd_ComponentWidthMismatch = 相连元件位宽不一致
+NetList_BitwidthError = 发现位宽连接问题
+NetList_CircuitGated = ----> 门控实例
+NetList_CircuitGatedNotGated = 同一电路同时用于门控时钟与非门控时钟，仅支持最简单情况，详见下方追踪列表
comment.body:
```suggestion
NetList_CircuitGatedNotGated = 同一电路同时使用了门控与非门控的时钟组件（详见下方追踪列表），仅支持最简单情况
```

-HDL_Tristate =找到一个或多个组件的三态驱动程序或浮动输出。这不受支持。
-HDL_unsupported =在电路中发现一个或多个不支持 HDL 生成的组件。
-MultipleSheetSameName =在您的设计中找到多张名称为“%s”的工作表。这是不允许的，请确保所有工作表都有唯一的名称！
-NetAdd_ComponentWidthMismatch =发现连接在一起的两个组件具有不同的位宽。
-NetList_BitwidthError =发现位宽连接问题。
-NetList_CircuitGated =->门控实例
-NetList_CircuitGatedNotGated =找到与选通和非选通时钟组件一起使用的电路（请参阅下面的跟踪列表）。这只适用于支持的最简单的情况！
-NetList_CircuitNotGated =->非门控实例
-NetList_duplicatedSplitter =在同一位置发现相同的分离器。
-NetList_emptynets =发现未连接的网络！
-NetList_GatedClock =找到了一个门控时钟。请小心，真正的硬件可能无法正常工作！
-NetList_GatedClockInt =->中间选通时钟线
-NetList_GatedClockSink =->选通时钟线的接收器
-NetList_GatedClockSource =->门控时钟线源
-NetList_IOError =找到一个或多个具有 I/O 针脚的组件，这不受支持。
-NetList_NoClockConnection =找到时钟输入未连接的组件！
-NetList_NoEndSplitterConnections =找到带有一个或多个针脚的分离器，这些针脚连接到网络，但没有连接到根总线。
-NetList_NoSplitterConnection =发现不能将信号从总线传输到扇出的分离器。
-NetList_NoSplitterEndConnections =发现具有一个或多个未连接的扇出针脚的分离器。
-NetList_PossibleGatedClock =在顶层发现可能的门控时钟！请小心，真正的硬件可能无法正常工作！
-NetList_ShortCircuit =找到具有多个驱动器的网络（短路）。
-NetList_SourceWithoutSink =找到没有（A）个接收器的源！
-# ==> NetList_TraceListBegin =
-# ==> NetList_TraceListEnd =
-NetList_UnconnectedInput =发现未连接的输入（位）！
-NetList_UnconnectedInputs =找到输入未连接的组件！
-NetList_UnconnectedOutput =发现未连接的输出（位）！
-NetList_UnsourcedSink =发现了一个无源的水槽！
-NetListBuild =正在为电路“%s”（%d/7）构建网表
-NetMerge_BitWidthError =正在尝试合并不同大小的网络。
-TopLevelNoIO =顶层“%s”没有输入和/或输出！
+BuildingNetlistFor = 正在为页面“%s”构建网表
+CircuitInfoString = 电路“%s”包含 %d 条网络和 %d 条总线
+DRCPassesString = 电路“%s”通过 DRC 检查
+EmptyNamedSheet = 页面名称不得为空，请指定名称
+FoundBadComponent = 在电路“%s”中发现元件“%s”
+HDL_CompNameIsLabel = 元件标签不得与电路名称相同
+HDL_DuplicatedLabels = 发现重复标签，请确保标签名称唯一
+HDL_LabelInvalid = 发现无效标签
+HDL_noLabel = 存在未设置标签的元件，请为其设置标签或使用自动标注功能
+HDL_Tristate = 不支持三态驱动或悬空输出
+HDL_unsupported = 电路中存在不支持生成 HDL 的元件

-EmptyNamedSheet =在您的设计中找到一个名称为空的工作表。这是不允许的，请指定名称！
-FoundBadComponent =发现电路“%2$s”中的组件“%1$s”
-HDL_CompNameIsLabel =找到标签等于电路名称的一个或多个组件。这不受支持。
-HDL_DuplicatedLabels =找到一个或多个重复的标签。请使标签名称唯一。
-HDL_LabelInvalid =发现无效的标签。
-HDL_noLabel =找到一个或多个没有标签的组件。请给它们贴上标签或使用批注功能。
-HDL_Tristate =找到一个或多个组件的三态驱动程序或浮动输出。这不受支持。
-HDL_unsupported =在电路中发现一个或多个不支持 HDL 生成的组件。
-MultipleSheetSameName =在您的设计中找到多张名称为“%s”的工作表。这是不允许的，请确保所有工作表都有唯一的名称！
-NetAdd_ComponentWidthMismatch =发现连接在一起的两个组件具有不同的位宽。
-NetList_BitwidthError =发现位宽连接问题。
-NetList_CircuitGated =->门控实例
-NetList_CircuitGatedNotGated =找到与选通和非选通时钟组件一起使用的电路（请参阅下面的跟踪列表）。这只适用于支持的最简单的情况！
-NetList_CircuitNotGated =->非门控实例
-NetList_duplicatedSplitter =在同一位置发现相同的分离器。
-NetList_emptynets =发现未连接的网络！
-NetList_GatedClock =找到了一个门控时钟。请小心，真正的硬件可能无法正常工作！
-NetList_GatedClockInt =->中间选通时钟线
-NetList_GatedClockSink =->选通时钟线的接收器
-NetList_GatedClockSource =->门控时钟线源
-NetList_IOError =找到一个或多个具有 I/O 针脚的组件，这不受支持。
-NetList_NoClockConnection =找到时钟输入未连接的组件！
-NetList_NoEndSplitterConnections =找到带有一个或多个针脚的分离器，这些针脚连接到网络，但没有连接到根总线。
-NetList_NoSplitterConnection =发现不能将信号从总线传输到扇出的分离器。
-NetList_NoSplitterEndConnections =发现具有一个或多个未连接的扇出针脚的分离器。
-NetList_PossibleGatedClock =在顶层发现可能的门控时钟！请小心，真正的硬件可能无法正常工作！
-NetList_ShortCircuit =找到具有多个驱动器的网络（短路）。
-NetList_SourceWithoutSink =找到没有（A）个接收器的源！
-# ==> NetList_TraceListBegin =
