issue.title: mach: Revert `--no-project` in `uv run`

labels.name: S-awaiting-review
labels.description: There is new code that needs to be reviewed.

issue.body:
`--no-project` was added as a temporary workaround in https://github.com/servo/servo/pull/37741.
It's not 100% clear to me what exactly the issue was, but [apparently](https://github.com/servo/servo/pull/37741#pullrequestreview-2985666234) the issue caused the build to break.
Removing the arg seems to work fine, except that we get a warning about a missing `requiress-python` value in `pyproject.toml`.
Apparently it is good practice to specify the requirement as `>=` in th pyroject, and lock the exact version via `uv pin` (which writes to `.python_version`, where we already pin 3.11.


Testing: Should be covered by existing tests, which compile code on all platforms.

comment.body:
Two of the requirements.txt files, come from upstream wpt, so I don't think we can / should move those to pyproject - Otherwise we would need to ensure our pyproject.toml stays in sync with the upstream requirements.txt. 
I think using `uv` to `uv pip install` should perhaps be left as is, but imho we could leave `uv` in charge of activating the venv.

> Moving to pyproject.tomlas the source of truth should allow supporting other venv managers, I think.

I think we would need to remove `uv run python` usage from our build-scripts. I think we should be able to assume that the build-system already activated the virtual environment. Perhaps we could try both `python3` and `python`, if windows not having `python3` was the only problem that `uv run python` solved.

labels.name: S-awaiting-review

issue.body:
`--no-project` was added as a temporary workaround in https://github.com/servo/servo/pull/37741.
It's not 100% clear to me what exactly the issue was, but [apparently](https://github.com/servo/servo/pull/37741#pullrequestreview-2985666234) the issue caused the build to break.
Removing the arg seems to work fine, except that we get a warning about a missing `requiress-python` value in `pyproject.toml`.
Apparently it is good practice to specify the requirement as `>=` in th pyroject, and lock the exact version via `uv pin` (which writes to `.python_version`, where we already pin 3.11.

Could you clarify what you mean with fixing the logic in mach? Would you want to define the version (3.11) in mach?

issue.title: Upgrade `cargo-deny` to `0.19.0` on self-hosted runners.
issue.body:
The versions older than 0.18.6 have a bug which causes them to error out prematurely. Servo's `mach test-tidy` currently doesn't catch this issue so we are silently allow patches that would otherwise fail `cargo-deny` to land.

After https://github.com/servo/servo/pull/41853 lands, `./mach test-tidy` will start failing if the self-hosted runner's cargo-deny version is older than 0.18.6. So, we should ensure the self-hosted runners move the 0.19.0 to match the github hosted runners.

comment.body: @mukilan We could install cargo-deny as part of the workflow (even when running on self-hosted runners), until we get the image updated.

issue.title: CI: Make HarmonyOS build job mandatory
issue.body:
This job was previously optional, since it doesn't have a fallback to github-hosted runners. However, this doesn't change much, since in practice if the runners are down, the merge queue will still wait for the job to complete (or fail the job after **24 hours**).

> If a job is labeled for a certain type of runner, but none matching that type are available, the job does not immediately fail at the time of queueing. Instead, the job will remain queued until the 24 hour timeout period expires.

See [self-hosted runners reference](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/runners/self-hosted-runners)

On the other side, making this job mandatory, will also check the tracing feature builds fine (at least on ohos), since it is the only configuration that currently uses tracing when building. This would have prevented https://github.com/servo/servo/issues/41802

Testing: Not required / this is a ci configuration change.

comment.body:
> Hm, maybe we should use runner timeout?

I tried that in https://github.com/servo/servo/pull/38044, but kept running into [token issues when merging](https://github.com/servo/servo/pull/38044#discussion_r2224689698).

Overall, I do think we will anyway want to have mandatory self-hosted jobs soonish anyway (probably with the mitigation mechanism that the jobs can be easily disabled, if there are problems with the self-hosted runners, like the half-yearly token swap).

issue.title: Better error message if libservo is compiled without vello and vello_cpu

labels.name: I-papercut
labels.description: Small but painful.

labels.name: A-build
labels.description: Related to or part of the build process

issue.body:
Currently either the vello or vello_cpu must be selected otherwise a compile error occurs in canvas. 
This usually doesn't come up, since the vello_cpu feature is unconditionally enabled by us. But since it is a feature flag, we should add a way to disable it (with no-default-features). The compile errors are a bit ugly if no backend is selected, so we should at least add a `compile_error!()` with a useful message.

comment.body:
> It's not an error to enable both at the same time. In fact, it is really useful as a GPU canvas implementation evolves to be able to use GPU canvas when you know it is fast and then fall back to the CPU version

That makes a lot of sense to me.

> I left a comment on https://github.com/servo/servo/pull/41698 about this, but essentially if we are working toward being able to build Servo with just cargo, it would be preferable to remove the compilation option for vello_cpu.

Quoting [your comment from #41686](https://github.com/servo/servo/pull/41686#issuecomment-3714316728) to keep the discussion together. 
I think there are some trade-offs here. Having vello_cpu as a fallback is certainly preferable for most cases, but in the context of an embedder customizing servo, an embedder could apply patches to servo (to e.g. provide a custom implementation, in which case `vello_cpu` might just be unneeded. Of course if the embedder patches, they could also patch `vello_cpu` out, but it might be easier, if we already have the option to disable it in the first place. 
I'm not strongly opposing removing the feature, just trying to say that there could be benefits in keeping the option to turn all canvas implementations off.

issue.title: Add Prof. Chengzhi Mao to Rutgers
issue.body:
# Contributing to CSrankings

Thanks for contributing to CSrankings! Please read and indicate you agree with **all** these guidelines to getting your pull request accepted. Note that pull requests may take some time to get merged (please don't contact us for at least three months for updates, unless you are a sponsor - see below).

**If you find CSrankings useful, [please consider becoming a sponsor](https://github.com/sponsors/csrankings).**

**NOTE:** IF YOU DO NOT FOLLOW THE STEPS BELOW, YOUR PULL REQUEST WILL BE SUMMARILY REJECTED.
You must read and check **all** the boxes below by filling them in with an X or your PR will be rejected.

**The Basics**

- [x] All pull requests and issues must come from non-anonymous accounts. Make sure your GitHub profile contains your full name.

- [x] Use a reasonable title that explains what the PR corresponds to (as in, not "Update csrankings-x.csv").

- [x] Combine multiple updates to a single institution into a **single PR.**

- [x] Only submit one pull request per institution.

- [x] Do not modify any files except `csrankings-[a-z].csv` or (if needed) `country-info.csv` and `old/industry.csv` (see below).

- [x] Do not use Excel to edit any .csv files; Excel incorrectly tries to
convert some Google Scholar entries to formulas, corrupting the
database. Use the GitHub user interface or a text editor like emacs or NotePad instead.

- [x] Insert new faculty **in alphabetical order** (not at the end) in the appropriate `csrankings-[a-z].csv` files. **Do not modify `csrankings.csv`, which is auto-generated.**

- [x] Check to make sure that you have no spaces after commas, or any missing fields.

- [x] Check to make sure the home page is correct.

- [x] Make sure the Google Scholar IDs are just the alphanumeric identifier (not a URL or with `&hl=en`).

- [x] Check to make sure the name corresponds to the DBLP entry (look it up at http://dblp.org).

- [x] If a faculty member is not in a CS department or similar, include a comment explaining how they meet the inclusion criteria (see below).

**Inclusion criteria**

- [x] Make sure that any faculty you add meet the inclusion
criteria. Eligible faculty include only full-time, tenure-track research
faculty members on a given campus who can *solely* advise PhD students in
Computer Science. Faculty not in a CS department or similar who can
advise PhD students in CS can be included regardless of their home
department. **Provide justification, pointing to specific home pages
showing how faculty not in a CS department meet the inclusion criteria,
e.g. showing a courtesy appointment in CS.** Faculty must also have a 75%+ time appointment (check
`old/industry.csv` for faculty who are now more than 25% in industry).

**Updating an affiliation or home page**

- [x] Update affiliations, home pages, and Google Scholar entries by modifying `csrankings-[a-z].csv`. For the Google Scholar entry, just use the alphanumeric identifier in the middle of the URL. If none is there, put `NOSCHOLARPAGE`.

**Adding one or more faculty members (including an entire department)**

- [x] If the department is not yet listed in CSrankings, the entire CS faculty needs to be added (not just one faculty member).

- [x] Enter each faculty member's [DBLP](http://dblp.org) name, home page, and Google Scholar entry (just the alphanumeric identifier, not the whole URL) by modifying `csrankings-[a-z].csv` (**the letters correspond to the first letter of the faculty members' names**); include disambiguation suffixes like `0001` as needed. If the faculty entry is currently ambiguous, please do not include them. Send mail to the DBLP maintainers (dblp@dagstuhl.de) with a few publications by a particular faculty member; also, open an issue so that when the DBLP database is updated, that faculty member's information can be added.

- [x] If DBLP has multiple entries for this person, *all of them need to be listed*. Do not update `dblp-aliases.csv`.

- [x] If the institution you are adding is not in the US,
update `country-info.csv` and add *all* of the faculty in the CS department.

**(Advanced) Quick contribution via a shallow clone** 

We recommend that you use the GitHub web user interface to make changes. However, it may be more convenient
to clone the repository for larger-scale changes.

However, a full clone of the CSrankings repository is almost 2GB.  To
contribute a change without creating a full local clone of the
CSrankings repo, you can perform a _shallow clone_.  To do so, follow these
steps:

1. Fork the CSrankings repo.  If you have an existing fork, but it is not up to date with the main repository, this technique may not work.  If necessary, delete and re-create your fork to get it up to date.  (Do **not** delete your existing fork if it has unmerged changes you want to preserve!)

1. Do a shallow clone of your fork: `git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/yourusername/CSrankings`.  This will only download the most recent commit, not the full git history.

1. Make your changes on a branch, push them to your clone, and create a pull request on GitHub as usual.

If you want to make another contribution and some time has passed, perform these steps again, creating a fresh fork and shallow clone.

issue.title: feat: Add DeepSpeed SFT support and vLLM server mode for reward model
issue.body: ## Summary This PR adds two major features (DeepSpeed SFT support and vLLM Server mode for RL reward model), along with several quality-of-life improvements for long-running training jobs. --- ## Major Features ### 1. DeepSpeed SFT Support (`op_compute_language_loss` override) The SFT pipeline was originally designed for Megatron strategy, where the model receives `labels` and returns per-token losses directly. In DeepSpeed strategy with HuggingFace models, the model returns logits instead, requiring different loss computation. **Background - Megatron vs DeepSpeed difference:** | | Megatron | DeepSpeed | |---|---|---| | Model input | `input_ids` + `labels` | `input_ids` only | | Model output | per-token losses | logits | | `op_compute_language_loss` receives | losses (just mask + sum) | logits (need cross-entropy) | **Solution:** Override `op_compute_language_loss` in `DeepSpeedTrainStrategy`: ```python def op_compute_language_loss(self, logits: torch.Tensor, labels: torch.Tensor): """ Override for DeepSpeed: compute cross-entropy from logits. Note: DataCollatorForSFT already shifts labels (shift_feature=True), so logits and labels are already aligned. Do NOT shift again. """ loss = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy( logits.view(-1, logits.size(-1)), labels.view(-1), ignore_index=IGNORE_INDEX ) return loss ``` **Why this approach:** - Keeps `SFTWorker.loss_func` generic (calls `strategy.op_compute_language_loss`) - Backend-specific loss computation is implemented in the corresponding Strategy class - Follows ROLL's design pattern where Strategy handles backend differences - `DataCollatorForSFT` default `shift_feature=True` already handles label shifting, so no double-shift needed **Files Changed:** - `roll/distributed/strategy/deepspeed_strategy.py`: Added `op_compute_language_loss` override - `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_worker.py`: Uses generic `op_compute_language_loss` interface --- ### 2. vLLM HTTP Server Mode for Reward Model #### Problem Statement In ROLL's async RL training, the reward model (used to score generated responses) becomes a significant bottleneck: - **Low GPU utilization**: Each reward worker loads its own model copy and processes requests sequentially - **Inefficient batching**: Even with multiple workers via `world_size`, there's no batching *within* each worker - **Resource waste**: Rollout GPU finishes fast and idles while reward computation lags behind #### Key Insight: Leveraging ROLL's Existing 256 Concurrency Before designing our solution, we analyzed ROLL's async scheduling mechanism in `async_generate_scheduler.py`: ```python # roll/distributed/scheduler/async_generate_scheduler.py, Line 316 @ray.remote(concurrency_groups={"single_thread": 1, "multi_thread": 256}) class AsyncDynamicSamplingScheduler: ... # Line 640-641: report_response uses the 256-thread concurrency group @ray.method(concurrency_group="multi_thread") def report_response(self, data: DataProto): ... # Line 654: Round-robin select a reward worker reward_worker = next(self.reward_worker_iters[experience_item.domain]) # Line 657: Call compute_rewards (this is where inference happens) rewards: DataProto = ray.get(reward_worker.compute_rewards.remote(batch)) ``` **observation**: ROLL already has 256 concurrent threads calling `report_response()`. Each call triggers a `compute_rewards()` on a reward worker. This means up to 256 reward computation requests can be *in flight* simultaneously. The problem is that each worker processes its request independently with its own model copy. Our solution: **centralize inference in a vLLM server that can batch all concurrent requests together**. #### Design Philosophy Our design follows these principles: 1. **Minimal modification**: Don't change ROLL's scheduling logic. Leverage the existing 256 concurrency. 2. **Sync over async**: Use simple synchronous HTTP requests. The concurrency comes from Ray's thread pool, not from async I/O complexity. 3. **Automatic lifecycle**: Pipeline manages server startup/shutdown. No manual intervention. 4. **Transparent batching**: vLLM handles batching internally. Workers just send requests. #### Architecture ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ AsyncDynamicSamplingScheduler (ROLL Original - Unchanged) │ │ │ │ @ray.remote(concurrency_groups={"multi_thread": 256}) │ │ │ │ When a rollout response completes, report_response() is called. │ │ Up to 256 report_response() calls can execute concurrently. │ │ │ │ report_response() #0 ─┬─► reward_worker.compute_rewards() ──┐ │ │ report_response() #1 ─┤ │ │ │ report_response() #2 ─┤ (round-robin dispatch to workers) │ │ │ ... │ │ │ │ report_response() #255 ┘ │ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┘ │ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Reward Workers (world_size = N) │ │ │ │ In vllm_server mode, workers become lightweight HTTP clients: │ │ - No GPU memory needed (model runs on vLLM server) │ │ - Can scale world_size freely to increase concurrency │ │ │ │ Each worker's compute_rewards() calls _vllm_server_inference(): │ │ │ │ def _vllm_server_inference(self, prompt: str) -> str: │ │ payload = { │ │ "model": self.vllm_model_name, │ │ "prompt": prompt, │ │ "max_tokens": max_tokens, │ │ "temperature": temperature, │ │ ... │ │ } │ │ response = requests.post( # Simple sync HTTP │ │ f"{self.vllm_server_url}/v1/completions", │ │ json=payload, │ │ timeout=120 │ │ ) │ │ return response.json()["choices"][0]["text"] │ │ │ │ Worker 0: requests.post(...) ───────┐ │ │ Worker 1: requests.post(...) ───────┤ │ │ Worker 2: requests.post(...) ───────┼───► HTTP requests arrive concurrently │ │ ... │ │ │ Worker N: requests.post(...) ───────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ vLLM HTTP Server (Dedicated GPU) │ │ │ │ Started automatically by RLVRPipeline._maybe_start_vllm_server() │ │ before training begins. Stopped automatically when training completes. │ │ │ │ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ vLLM Continuous Batching Engine │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ Multiple HTTP requests arrive concurrently: │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ Request 0 ──┐ │ │ │ │ Request 1 ──┼──► Dynamic Batching ──► Single Batched GPU Forward Pass │ │ │ │ Request 2 ──┤ │ │ │ │ │ ... │ │ │ │ │ │ Request N ──┘ ▼ │ │ │ │ Results dispatched back to each request │ │ │ │ (HTTP protocol handles request-response matching) │ │ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ Key benefit: All concurrent requests are batched together for GPU inference, │ │ achieving high GPU utilization regardless of how many workers there are. │ │ │ │ Server logs: /tmp/vllm_server_{port}.log │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` #### Implementation Details **1. Configuration (`roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_config.py`)** Added new fields to `RewardConfig`: ```python @dataclass class RewardConfig: judge_model_type: str = "local" # "local", "vllm", or "vllm_server" # vLLM server mode config vllm_server_url: Optional[str] = None # Auto-generated if not provided vllm_server_gpu: Optional[int] = None # GPU index for server vllm_server_port: int = 8000 vllm_server_gpu_memory_utilization: float = 0.85 vllm_server_max_model_len: int = 8192 ``` **2. Server Lifecycle (`roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_pipeline.py`)** Pipeline automatically manages vLLM server: ```python class RLVRPipeline: def _maybe_start_vllm_server(self): """Start vLLM server if any reward config uses vllm_server mode.""" for domain, reward_config in self.config.rewards.items(): if reward_config.judge_model_type == "vllm_server": self.vllm_server_manager = VLLMServerManager( model_path=reward_config.model_args.model_name_or_path, gpu_id=reward_config.vllm_server_gpu, port=reward_config.vllm_server_port, gpu_memory_utilization=reward_config.vllm_server_gpu_memory_utilization, max_model_len=reward_config.vllm_server_max_model_len, ) self.vllm_server_manager.start() # Blocks until server is ready break def run(self): self._maybe_start_vllm_server() try: # ... training loop finally: if self.vllm_server_manager: self.vllm_server_manager.stop() ``` **3. Server Manager (`roll/utils/vllm_server.py`)** Handles subprocess management for vLLM: ```python class VLLMServerManager: def start(self): """Start vLLM server as subprocess and wait until ready.""" cmd = [ "python", "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server", "--model", self.model_path, "--port", str(self.port), "--gpu-memory-utilization", str(self.gpu_memory_utilization), "--max-model-len", str(self.max_model_len), ] env = os.environ.copy() env["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = str(self.gpu_id) # Redirect stdout to file to avoid pipe buffer blocking (see Bug Fix 2) self._log_file = open(f"/tmp/vllm_server_{self.port}.log", "w") self.process = subprocess.Popen( cmd, stdout=self._log_file, stderr=subprocess.STDOUT, env=env, ) self._wait_until_ready() # Poll health endpoint until server responds def stop(self): """Gracefully stop the vLLM server.""" if self.process: self.process.terminate() self.process.wait(timeout=30) ``` **4. Worker Inference (`roll/pipeline/rlvr/rewards/proof_verifier_reward_worker.py`)** Simple sync HTTP client: ```python class ProofVerifierRewardWorker: def initialize(self, reward_config: RewardConfig): if reward_config.judge_model_type == "vllm_server": self.use_vllm_server = True self.vllm_server_url = reward_config.vllm_server_url or \ f"http://localhost:{reward_config.vllm_server_port}" self.vllm_model_name = reward_config.model_args.model_name_or_path def _vllm_server_inference(self, prompt: str) -> str: """Send inference request to vLLM server.""" payload = { "model": self.vllm_model_name, "prompt": prompt, "max_tokens": self.generation_config.get("max_new_tokens", 2048), "temperature": self.generation_config.get("temperature", 0.6), "top_p": self.generation_config.get("top_p", 0.95), "repetition_penalty": self.generation_config.get("repetition_penalty", 1.0), } response = requests.post( f"{self.vllm_server_url}/v1/completions", json=payload, timeout=120, ) response.raise_for_status() return response.json()["choices"][0]["text"] def compute_rewards(self, batch: DataProto) -> DataProto: # ... prepare prompt if self.use_vllm_server: llm_response = self._vllm_server_inference(prompt) else: llm_response = self._local_inference(prompt) # ... extract score from response ``` #### Why Sync HTTP Instead of Async? A natural question: why use synchronous `requests.post()` instead of async HTTP with `aiohttp`? The concurrency already exists at the Ray level. Each of the 256 concurrent `report_response()` calls runs in its own thread. When a worker calls `requests.post()`, that thread blocks waiting for the response, but other threads continue sending their requests. From vLLM server's perspective, it receives multiple concurrent HTTP connections regardless of whether clients use sync or async I/O. Using async would add complexity (event loops, coroutines, error handling) without improving throughput. The sync approach is simpler and equally effective. #### What We Changed vs. ROLL Original | Component | ROLL Original | Our Changes | |-----------|---------------|-------------| | `report_response()` 256 concurrency | ✅ Exists | Unchanged | | Round-robin worker dispatch | ✅ Exists | Unchanged | | `compute_rewards()` interface | ✅ Exists | Unchanged | | `_vllm_server_inference()` | ❌ | ✅ Added | | `VLLMServerManager` | ❌ | ✅ Added | | `_maybe_start_vllm_server()` | ❌ | ✅ Added | | vLLM server config fields | ❌ | ✅ Added | #### Usage ```yaml rewards: proof_gen: judge_model_type: vllm_server vllm_server_gpu: 3 # GPU to run server on (auto-start) vllm_server_port: 8000 # Server port vllm_server_gpu_memory_utilization: 0.85 vllm_server_max_model_len: 8192 world_size: 8 # Can scale up freely model_args: model_name_or_path: ${reward_pretrain} generating_args: max_new_tokens: 2048 temperature: 0.6 repetition_penalty: 1.1 ``` | Field | Description | Default | |-------|-------------|---------| | `judge_model_type` | Must be `vllm_server` | - | | `vllm_server_gpu` | GPU index for server | Required | | `vllm_server_port` | Server port | 8000 | | `vllm_server_gpu_memory_utilization` | GPU memory utilization | 0.85 | | `vllm_server_max_model_len` | Max context length | 8192 | | `world_size` | Number of HTTP client workers | 1 | #### Debugging 1. **Check server startup**: Look for `initialized with vLLM server mode: http://localhost:8000` in logs 2. **Server health check**: `curl http://localhost:8000/health` 3. **Manual inference test**: ```bash curl http://localhost:8000/v1/completions \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"model": "/path/to/model", "prompt": "Hello", "max_tokens": 10}' ``` 4. **Check server logs**: `tail -f /tmp/vllm_server_8000.log` 5. **Monitor GPU**: vLLM server GPU should show high utilization during reward computation #### Files Changed | File | Changes | |------|---------| | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_config.py` | Added vLLM server config fields to `RewardConfig` | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_pipeline.py` | Added `_maybe_start_vllm_server()` for automatic server lifecycle | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rewards/proof_verifier_reward_worker.py` | Added `_vllm_server_inference()` with sync HTTP | | `roll/utils/vllm_server.py` | New file - `VLLMServerManager` class | --- ## Quality-of-Life Features ### 3. Automatic Checkpoint Cleanup (`max_ckpt_to_keep`) Long training runs can generate many checkpoints and exhaust disk space. This adds a built-in mechanism to limit checkpoint count. **Usage:** ```yaml save_steps: 100 max_ckpt_to_keep: 3 # Keep only the latest 3 checkpoints, 0 = keep all ``` **How It Works:** 1. After each checkpoint save, scan `output_dir` for all `checkpoint-{step}` directories 2. Sort by step number, keep the latest N, delete older ones **Files Changed:** - `roll/configs/base_config.py`: New `max_ckpt_to_keep` parameter - `roll/pipeline/base_pipeline.py`: New `_cleanup_old_checkpoints()` method --- ### 4. Wandb Offline Mode Support Training on clusters without internet access cannot use wandb logging. Added `mode` parameter to `WandbTracker`. **Usage:** ```yaml tracker_kwargs: mode: offline # Can be "online", "offline", or "disabled" ``` **Files Changed:** - `roll/utils/tracking.py`: Added `mode` parameter to `WandbTracker` --- ### 5. Tqdm Progress Bar for SFT Training Added tqdm progress bar to `sft_pipeline.py` for better training visualization. **Files Changed:** - `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_pipeline.py`: Added tqdm progress bar --- ### 6. `setup.py` for pip Installation Added `setup.py` to support editable installation: ```bash cd ROLL pip install -e . ``` **Files Changed:** - `setup.py`: New file --- ## Bug Fixes ### Fix 1: SFT DataLoader Shuffle Changed `shuffle=False` to `shuffle=True` in SFT DataLoader for proper training data shuffling. **Files Changed:** - `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_pipeline.py`: Fixed shuffle parameter --- ### Fix 2: vLLM Server Stdout Pipe Buffer Blocking **Problem:** vLLM server was started with `stdout=subprocess.PIPE`, but the main process never read from the pipe. When vLLM outputs enough logs to fill the pipe buffer (~64KB), the server process blocks on `write()` and stops responding to HTTP requests. **Symptom:** First few requests succeed, then all subsequent requests timeout. GPU utilization drops to 0%. **Fix:** Redirect stdout to a log file instead of pipe: ```python # Before (blocking) self.process = subprocess.Popen(cmd, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, ...) # After (non-blocking) self._log_file_handle = open(f"/tmp/vllm_server_{port}.log", "w") self.process = subprocess.Popen(cmd, stdout=self._log_file_handle, ...) ``` **Files Changed:** - `roll/utils/vllm_server.py`: Fixed stdout handling --- ## Files Changed Summary | File | Changes | |------|---------| | `roll/configs/base_config.py` | Add `max_ckpt_to_keep` parameter | | `roll/pipeline/base_pipeline.py` | Add `_cleanup_old_checkpoints()` method | | `roll/utils/tracking.py` | Add wandb offline mode support | | `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_pipeline.py` | Add tqdm progress bar, fix shuffle | | `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_worker.py` | Use generic `op_compute_language_loss` interface | | `roll/distributed/strategy/deepspeed_strategy.py` | Add `op_compute_language_loss` override for SFT | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_config.py` | Add vLLM server config fields | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_pipeline.py` | Add `_maybe_start_vllm_server()` method | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rewards/` | Add vLLM server mode support | | `roll/utils/vllm_server.py` | New file - `VLLMServerManager` class | | `setup.py` | New file for pip install | --- ## Breaking Changes None. All changes are backward compatible. --- ## Testing - **DeepSpeed SFT:** Tested on Qwen3-4B model. Loss starts at expected range (indicating correct label alignment). - **vLLM Server Mode:** Tested async RL training with vLLM server mode. Server auto-starts, handles concurrent requests, and auto-stops on completion.

issue.title: feat: Add DeepSpeed SFT support and quality-of-life improvements
issue.body:
## DeepSpeed SFT Support

The SFT pipeline was originally designed for Megatron strategy, where the model receives labels and returns per-token losses directly. In DeepSpeed strategy with HuggingFace models, the model returns logits instead.

**Solution:** Override `op_compute_language_loss` in `DeepSpeedTrainStrategy`:
- Compute cross-entropy directly from logits
- DataCollatorForSFT already shifts labels (shift_feature=True by default), so logits and labels are already aligned - no double-shift needed

This follows ROLL's design pattern where Strategy handles backend differences, keeping Worker code generic.

## Quality-of-Life Features

1. **Checkpoint Cleanup (`max_ckpt_to_keep`)**
   - Automatically delete old checkpoints to prevent disk exhaustion
   - Usage: `max_ckpt_to_keep: 3` keeps only latest 3 checkpoints

2. **Wandb Offline Mode**
   - Added `mode` parameter to WandbTracker
   - Usage: `tracker_kwargs: {mode: offline}`

3. **SFT Training Improvements**
   - Enable data shuffling in DataLoader (was False)
   - Add tqdm progress bar for training visualization

4. **pip Installation Support**
   - Added setup.py for `pip install -e .`

Configuration (`roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_config.py`)** Added new fields to `RewardConfig`: ```python @dataclass class RewardConfig: judge_model_type: str = "local" # "local", "vllm", or "vllm_server" # vLLM server mode config vllm_server_url: Optional[str] = None # Auto-generated if not provided vllm_server_gpu: Optional[int] = None # GPU index for server vllm_server_port: int = 8000 vllm_server_gpu_memory_utilization: float = 0.85 vllm_server_max_model_len: int = 8192 ``` **2. Server Lifecycle (`roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_pipeline.py`)** Pipeline automatically manages vLLM server: ```python class RLVRPipeline: def _maybe_start_vllm_server(self): """Start vLLM server if any reward config uses vllm_server mode.""" for domain, reward_config in self.config.rewards.items(): if reward_config.judge_model_type == "vllm_server": self.vllm_server_manager = VLLMServerManager( model_path=reward_config.model_args.model_name_or_path, gpu_id=reward_config.vllm_server_gpu, port=reward_config.vllm_server_port, gpu_memory_utilization=reward_config.vllm_server_gpu_memory_utilization, max_model_len=reward_config.vllm_server_max_model_len, ) self.vllm_server_manager.start() # Blocks until server is ready break def run(self): self._maybe_start_vllm_server() try: # ... training loop finally: if self.vllm_server_manager: self.vllm_server_manager.stop() ``` **3. Server Manager (`roll/utils/vllm_server.py`)** Handles subprocess management for vLLM: ```python class VLLMServerManager: def start(self): """Start vLLM server as subprocess and wait until ready.""" cmd = [ "python", "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server", "--model", self.model_path, "--port", str(self.port), "--gpu-memory-utilization", str(self.gpu_memory_utilization), "--max-model-len", str(self.max_model_len), ] env = os.environ.copy() env["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = str(self.gpu_id) # Redirect stdout to file to avoid pipe buffer blocking (see Bug Fix 2) self._log_file = open(f"/tmp/vllm_server_{self.port}.log", "w") self.process = subprocess.Popen( cmd, stdout=self._log_file, stderr=subprocess.STDOUT, env=env, ) self._wait_until_ready() # Poll health endpoint until server responds def stop(self): """Gracefully stop the vLLM server.""" if self.process: self.process.terminate() self.process.wait(timeout=30) ``` **4. Worker Inference (`roll/pipeline/rlvr/rewards/proof_verifier_reward_worker.py`)** Simple sync HTTP client: ```python class ProofVerifierRewardWorker: def initialize(self, reward_config: RewardConfig): if reward_config.judge_model_type == "vllm_server": self.use_vllm_server = True self.vllm_server_url = reward_config.vllm_server_url or \ f"http://localhost:{reward_config.vllm_server_port}" self.vllm_model_name = reward_config.model_args.model_name_or_path def _vllm_server_inference(self, prompt: str) -> str: """Send inference request to vLLM server.""" payload = { "model": self.vllm_model_name, "prompt": prompt, "max_tokens": self.generation_config.get("max_new_tokens", 2048), "temperature": self.generation_config.get("temperature", 0.6), "top_p": self.generation_config.get("top_p", 0.95), "repetition_penalty": self.generation_config.get("repetition_penalty", 1.0), } response = requests.post( f"{self.vllm_server_url}/v1/completions", json=payload, timeout=120, ) response.raise_for_status() return response.json()["choices"][0]["text"] def compute_rewards(self, batch: DataProto) -> DataProto: # ... prepare prompt if self.use_vllm_server: llm_response = self._vllm_server_inference(prompt) else: llm_response = self._local_inference(prompt) # ... extract score from response ``` #### Why Sync HTTP Instead of Async? A natural question: why use synchronous `requests.post()` instead of async HTTP with `aiohttp`? The concurrency already exists at the Ray level. Each of the 256 concurrent `report_response()` calls runs in its own thread. When a worker calls `requests.post()`, that thread blocks waiting for the response, but other threads continue sending their requests. From vLLM server's perspective, it receives multiple concurrent HTTP connections regardless of whether clients use sync or async I/O. Using async would add complexity (event loops, coroutines, error handling) without improving throughput. The sync approach is simpler and equally effective. #### What We Changed vs. ROLL Original | Component | ROLL Original | Our Changes | |-----------|---------------|-------------| | `report_response()` 256 concurrency | ✅ Exists | Unchanged | | Round-robin worker dispatch | ✅ Exists | Unchanged | | `compute_rewards()` interface | ✅ Exists | Unchanged | | `_vllm_server_inference()` | ❌ | ✅ Added | | `VLLMServerManager` | ❌ | ✅ Added | | `_maybe_start_vllm_server()` | ❌ | ✅ Added | | vLLM server config fields | ❌ | ✅ Added | #### Usage ```yaml rewards: proof_gen: judge_model_type: vllm_server vllm_server_gpu: 3 # GPU to run server on (auto-start) vllm_server_port: 8000 # Server port vllm_server_gpu_memory_utilization: 0.85 vllm_server_max_model_len: 8192 world_size: 8 # Can scale up freely model_args: model_name_or_path: ${reward_pretrain} generating_args: max_new_tokens: 2048 temperature: 0.6 repetition_penalty: 1.1 ``` | Field | Description | Default | |-------|-------------|---------| | `judge_model_type` | Must be `vllm_server` | - | | `vllm_server_gpu` | GPU index for server | Required | | `vllm_server_port` | Server port | 8000 | | `vllm_server_gpu_memory_utilization` | GPU memory utilization | 0.85 | | `vllm_server_max_model_len` | Max context length | 8192 | | `world_size` | Number of HTTP client workers | 1 | #### Debugging 1. **Check server startup**: Look for `initialized with vLLM server mode: http://localhost:8000` in logs 2. **Server health check**: `curl http://localhost:8000/health` 3. **Manual inference test**: ```bash curl http://localhost:8000/v1/completions \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"model": "/path/to/model", "prompt": "Hello", "max_tokens": 10}' ``` 4. **Check server logs**: `tail -f /tmp/vllm_server_8000.log` 5. **Monitor GPU**: vLLM server GPU should show high utilization during reward computation #### Files Changed | File | Changes | |------|---------| | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_config.py` | Added vLLM server config fields to `RewardConfig` | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_pipeline.py` | Added `_maybe_start_vllm_server()` for automatic server lifecycle | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rewards/proof_verifier_reward_worker.py` | Added `_vllm_server_inference()` with sync HTTP | | `roll/utils/vllm_server.py` | New file - `VLLMServerManager` class | --- ## Quality-of-Life Features ### 3. Automatic Checkpoint Cleanup (`max_ckpt_to_keep`) Long training runs can generate many checkpoints and exhaust disk space. This adds a built-in mechanism to limit checkpoint count. **Usage:** ```yaml save_steps: 100 max_ckpt_to_keep: 3 # Keep only the latest 3 checkpoints, 0 = keep all ``` **How It Works:** 1. After each checkpoint save, scan `output_dir` for all `checkpoint-{step}` directories 2. Sort by step number, keep the latest N, delete older ones **Files Changed:** - `roll/configs/base_config.py`: New `max_ckpt_to_keep` parameter - `roll/pipeline/base_pipeline.py`: New `_cleanup_old_checkpoints()` method --- ### 4. Wandb Offline Mode Support Training on clusters without internet access cannot use wandb logging. Added `mode` parameter to `WandbTracker`. **Usage:** ```yaml tracker_kwargs: mode: offline # Can be "online", "offline", or "disabled" ``` **Files Changed:** - `roll/utils/tracking.py`: Added `mode` parameter to `WandbTracker` --- ### 5. Tqdm Progress Bar for SFT Training Added tqdm progress bar to `sft_pipeline.py` for better training visualization. **Files Changed:** - `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_pipeline.py`: Added tqdm progress bar --- ### 6. `setup.py` for pip Installation Added `setup.py` to support editable installation: ```bash cd ROLL pip install -e . ``` **Files Changed:** - `setup.py`: New file --- ## Bug Fixes ### Fix 1: SFT DataLoader Shuffle Changed `shuffle=False` to `shuffle=True` in SFT DataLoader for proper training data shuffling. **Files Changed:** - `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_pipeline.py`: Fixed shuffle parameter --- ### Fix 2: vLLM Server Stdout Pipe Buffer Blocking **Problem:** vLLM server was started with `stdout=subprocess.PIPE`, but the main process never read from the pipe. When vLLM outputs enough logs to fill the pipe buffer (~64KB), the server process blocks on `write()` and stops responding to HTTP requests. **Symptom:** First few requests succeed, then all subsequent requests timeout. GPU utilization drops to 0%. **Fix:** Redirect stdout to a log file instead of pipe: ```python # Before (blocking) self.process = subprocess.Popen(cmd, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, ...) # After (non-blocking) self._log_file_handle = open(f"/tmp/vllm_server_{port}.log", "w") self.process = subprocess.Popen(cmd, stdout=self._log_file_handle, ...) ``` **Files Changed:** - `roll/utils/vllm_server.py`: Fixed stdout handling --- ## Files Changed Summary | File | Changes | |------|---------| | `roll/configs/base_config.py` | Add `max_ckpt_to_keep` parameter | | `roll/pipeline/base_pipeline.py` | Add `_cleanup_old_checkpoints()` method | | `roll/utils/tracking.py` | Add wandb offline mode support | | `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_pipeline.py` | Add tqdm progress bar, fix shuffle | | `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_worker.py` | Use generic `op_compute_language_loss` interface | | `roll/distributed/strategy/deepspeed_strategy.py` | Add `op_compute_language_loss` override for SFT | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_config.py` | Add vLLM server config fields | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rlvr_pipeline.py` | Add `_maybe_start_vllm_server()` method | | `roll/pipeline/rlvr/rewards/` | Add vLLM server mode support | | `roll/utils/vllm_server.py` | New file - `VLLMServerManager` class | | `setup.py` | New file for pip install | --- ## Breaking Changes None. All changes are backward compatible. --- ## Testing - **DeepSpeed SFT:** Tested on Qwen3-4B model. Loss starts at expected range (indicating correct label alignment). - **vLLM Server Mode:** Tested async RL training with vLLM server mode. Server auto-starts, handles concurrent requests, and auto-stops on completion.

issue.title: Add SFT training features and fix SFT pipeline issues
issue.body: ## Summary This PR adds useful features for long-running training jobs and fixes some issues in the SFT pipeline. --- ## New Features ### 1. Add automatic checkpoint cleanup (`max_ckpt_to_keep`) Long training runs can generate many checkpoints and exhaust disk space. This adds a built-in mechanism to limit checkpoint count. **Changes:** - `roll/configs/base_config.py`: New `max_ckpt_to_keep` parameter - `roll/pipeline/base_pipeline.py`: New `_cleanup_old_checkpoints()` method **Usage:** ```yaml save_steps: 100 max_ckpt_to_keep: 3 # Keep only the latest 3 checkpoints, 0 = keep all ``` **How It Works:** 1. After each checkpoint save, scan `output_dir` for all subdirectories 2. Collect all `checkpoint-{step}` directories 3. Sort by step number, keep the latest N, delete older ones --- ### 2. Add wandb offline mode support Training on clusters without internet access cannot use wandb logging. Added `mode` parameter to `WandbTracker` to support offline logging. **Usage:** ```yaml tracker_kwargs: mode: offline # Can be "online", "offline", or "disabled" ``` --- ### 3. Add tqdm progress bar for SFT training Added tqdm progress bar to `sft_pipeline.py` for better training visualization. --- ### 4. Add `setup.py` for pip installation Added `setup.py` to support editable installation: ```bash cd ROLL pip install -e . ``` --- ## Bug Fixes ### 5. Fix loss function call in `sft_worker.py` The original code calls `op_compute_language_loss(output_tensor, labels)`, but `output_tensor` is actually logits, while `op_compute_language_loss` expects pre-computed per-token losses. **Fix:** Use `op_compute_language_loss_from_logits` which correctly computes cross-entropy loss from logits. ```python def loss_func(self, data: DataProto, output_tensor: torch.Tensor): labels = data.batch["labels"] loss, _ = self.strategy.op_compute_language_loss_from_logits(output_tensor, labels) metrics = {f"{self.worker_config.name}/loss": loss.detach().float().unsqueeze(0)} return loss, metrics ``` Note: `.float()` conversion is needed because bf16 tensors don't support numpy conversion. --- ### 6. Fix double-shift issue in `sft_pipeline.py` After applying fix #5, labels get shifted twice: 1. `DataCollatorForSFT` with `shift_feature=True` (default) 2. Inside `op_compute_language_loss_from_logits` This causes misalignment between logits and labels. **Fix:** Set `shift_feature=False` since the loss function already handles shifting: ```python data_collator = DataCollatorForSFT( ... shift_feature=False, # loss func already does shift ) ``` --- ## Files Changed | File | Changes | |------|---------| | `roll/configs/base_config.py` | Add `max_ckpt_to_keep` parameter | | `roll/pipeline/base_pipeline.py` | Add checkpoint cleanup | | `roll/utils/tracking.py` | Add wandb offline mode support | | `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_worker.py` | Fix loss computation | | `roll/pipeline/sft/sft_pipeline.py` | Fix double-shift issue, add tqdm progress bar | | `setup.py` | New file for pip install | --- ## Breaking Changes None. All changes are backward compatible.

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

issue.body:
<!-- ⚠️ Please DON'T ignore the issue template -->

<!-- 💡 Consider upgrading to the latest version before sending the issue -->

**Describe  the bug**

After changing the zoom level, the content after the page will overlap in advance when turning the page.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e2f6acbf-5f04-499f-8b95-ffd0ce6e109c)

**Minimal reproduction**

1. Create `./slides.md` :
```md
---
transition: slide-left
---

# This is the first page

---
zoom: 0.5
---

# This is the second page

---
zoom: 1.5
---

# This is the third page
```

2. Run `slidev --open` in terminal.
3. See the error in browser.

You can use https://sli.dev/new to create a new project to reproduce the issue.

**Environment**

- Slidev version: 51.7.1
- Browser: Edge
- OS: Windows 11

非常感谢帮忙更新翻译！

除了新添加的内容外，本PR似乎包含了对很多已有内容的重新翻译，应该是由于 Git 记录对不上导致无法辨别英文文档中修改了的部分。我 review 了 .vitepress/\*，guide/\* 和所有（子）标题，选择了旧翻译和新翻译中我认为较好的版本。其他变更由于过多，我只粗略地浏览了一下，我觉得翻译质量和之前相比持平或略高一些，有细微的差错应该不影响读者理解，如有时间也请帮忙再核对一下，主要是 builtin/\*、custom/\*、features/\* 的正文部分。

我还在 README.md 和 guide/index.md 中添加了之前创建的 QQ 群号（978643067），这个群感觉可以一直运营下去，目前有 76 人。

此外，@normal-coder 能否在 README.md 中添加一下未来更新翻译的工作流程，以免之后忘记，非常感谢~

and 这个 PR 应该怎么 merge? 是不是不能 Squash。

labels.name: documentation

issue.title: SyntaxError: writeGlobalTypes export missing from @vue/language-core breaks twoslash-vue integration
issue.body:
**Describe the bug**

When creating a new Slidev project using `pnpm create slidev` and attempting to start the dev server, the application fails to start with a module import error. The error indicates that `twoslash-vue@0.3.4` is trying to import `writeGlobalTypes` from `@vue/language-core`, but this export does not exist in the installed version of `@vue/language-core`.

**Minimal reproduction**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Run `pnpm create slidev`
2. Enter project name (e.g., "pitch-deck")
3. When prompted to remove existing files, select "yes"
4. When prompted to install and start, select "yes"
5. Choose `pnpm` as the package manager
6. The dev server fails to start with the error below

You can use https://sli.dev/new to create a new project to reproduce the issue.

**Error Details**

```
file:///Users/kejvidoko/dev/byte-vision/pitch-deck/node_modules/.pnpm/twoslash-vue@0.3.4_typescript@5.9.3/node_modules/twoslash-vue/dist/index.mjs:1
import { defaultMapperFactory, CompilerOptionsResolver, writeGlobalTypes, createVueLanguagePlugin, createLanguage, FileMap } from '@vue/language-core';
                                                        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
SyntaxError: The requested module '@vue/language-core' does not provide an export named 'writeGlobalTypes'
    at #asyncInstantiate (node:internal/modules/esm/module_job:302:21)
    at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:103:5)
    at async ModuleJob.run (node:internal/modules/esm/module_job:405:5)
    at async onImport.tracePromise.__proto__ (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:654:26)
    at async Promise.all (index 2)
    at async getTwoslashTransformer (file:///Users/kejvidoko/dev/byte-vision/pitch-deck/node_modules/.pnpm/@slidev+cli@52.11.1_@babel+parser@7.28.5_@nuxt+kit@3.20.2_@types+markdown-it@14.1.2_@ty_2d3842277e856cbbfece37ba94ab0f42/node_modules/@slidev/cli/dist/shared-DgnE1q2H.mjs:1734:41)
    at async MarkdownItShiki (file:///Users/kejvidoko/dev/byte-vision/pitch-deck/node_modules/.pnpm/@slidev+cli@52.11.1_@babel+parser@7.28.5_@nuxt+kit@3.20.2_@types+markdown-it@14.1.2_@ty_2d3842277e856cbbfece37ba94ab0f42/node_modules/@slidev/cli/dist/shared-DgnE1q2H.mjs:1752:61)
    at async useMarkdownItPlugins (file:///Users/kejvidoko/dev/byte-vision/pitch-deck/node_modules/.pnpm/@slidev+cli@52.11.1_@babel+parser@7.28.5_@nuxt+kit@3.20.2_@types+markdown-it@14.1.2_@ty_2d3842277e856cbbfece37ba94ab0f42/node_modules/@slidev/cli/dist/shared-DgnE1q2H.mjs:1832:45)
    at async Object.markdownItSetup (file:///Users/kejvidoko/dev/byte-vision/pitch-deck/node_modules/.pnpm/@slidev+cli@52.11.1_@babel+parser@7.28.5_@nuxt+kit@3.20.2_@types+markdown-it@14.1.2_@ty_2d3842277e856cbbfece37ba94ab0f42/node_modules/@slidev/cli/dist/shared-DgnE1q2H.mjs:2225:4)
    at async file:///Users/kejvidoko/dev/byte-vision/pitch-deck/node_modules/.pnpm/unplugin-vue-markdown@29.2.0_vite@7.3.0_@types+node@22.19.3_jiti@2.6.1_yaml@2.8.2_/node_modules/unplugin-vue-markdown/dist/chunk-WO5APWOV.js:142:5

Node.js v25.2.1
 ELIFECYCLE  Command failed with exit code 1.
```

The error originates from:
- `twoslash-vue@0.3.4` attempting to import `writeGlobalTypes` from `@vue/language-core`
- The import statement: `import { defaultMapperFactory, CompilerOptionsResolver, writeGlobalTypes, createVueLanguagePlugin, createLanguage, FileMap } from '@vue/language-core';`

**Environment**

- Slidev version: `@slidev/cli@52.11.1`
- Node.js version: `v25.2.1`
- Package manager: `pnpm v10.22.0`
- OS: macOS (darwin 25.2.0)
- Browser: N/A (error occurs before server starts)

**Additional Context**

- This is a fresh project created using the Slidev CLI
- The project uses the default `seriph` theme
- The error occurs during the module initialization phase, before the dev server can start
- This appears to be a dependency compatibility issue between `twoslash-vue@0.3.4` and the resolved version of `@vue/language-core`

If you are using Slidev globally (i.e. `npx slidev` or `npm i -g slidev`), please try to reproduce the issue in a local project (i.e. `npm create slidev@latest`).

issue.title: Preparser.ts not loaded from theme
issue.body:
<!-- ⚠️ Please DON'T ignore the issue template -->

<!-- 💡 Consider upgrading to the latest version before sending the issue -->

**Describe the bug**

If I create a `setup/preparser.ts` in a theme, it gets ignored.
This might be related to https://github.com/slidevjs/slidev/issues/926

**Minimal reproduction**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Create a new slidev project in `apps/slides` via `pnpm create slidev`
2. Create a new theme in `packages/slidev-theme-mytheme` via `pnpm create slidev-theme`
3. In the theme, create a `setup/preparser.ts` besides the `setup/shiki.ts`
4. Add `console.log` to both setup files.
5. The `console.log` from the shiki file gets output, the one from the preparser file does not (also no hint that the rest of `preparser.ts` gets executed).

**Environment**

- Slidev version: 52.10.1
- Browser: Chrome
- OS: Mac

issue.title: \{\{ in KateX is mistakenly interpreted as a JS code.
issue.body:
<!-- ⚠️ Please DON'T ignore the issue template -->

<!-- 💡 Consider upgrading to the latest version before sending the issue -->

**Describe the bug**

Inside LaTeX math expressions in Slidev (`$...$` or `$$...$$`), any occurrence of `\{\{` is incorrectly treated by Vue's template compiler as the beginning of a mustache interpolation block (`{{ ... }}`).
As a result, the math formula breaks, parts of it disappear, or Slidev shows a compile error.

In LaTeX, `\{\{` is a valid way to typeset literal braces or appears inside certain macro uses, so it should be passed through to KaTeX/MathJax unchanged.
Instead, Slidev/Vite parses it too early as a Vue template expression.

**Expected behavior**

`\{\{` inside math mode should not be interpreted as Vue `{{ ... }}` interpolation and should be forwarded verbatim to the math engine.

---

**Minimal reproduction**

1. Create a new Slidev project (`npm create slidev@latest` or [https://sli.dev/new](https://sli.dev/new))

2. Add a slide with the following content:

   ```md
   $$
   f(x) = \{\{ x \}\}
   $$
   ```

3. Run `npm run dev`

4. The slide fails to render because Vue treats `{{ x }}` as a mustache expression instead of literal math.

---

**Environment**

* Slidev version: v52.4.0
* Browser: Chrome 
* OS: Windows 10

issue.title: perf(grouped-rmsnorm): fuse the calculation of dx and partial dgamma
issue.body:
Improve performance of `GroupedRMSNorm` by fusing the calculation of `dx` and partial `dgamma`.

/gemini review

comment.body:
## Benchmark Results

Grouped 32 `RMSNorm`, Hidden size 4096, Batch size 20480 (equal split).

### Baseline: A single global `torch.rms_norm`

| `dtype` | Backward Bandwidth (GB/s) |
| ------- | ------------------------- |
| `float32`  | 285.4 |
| `bfloat16` | 275.2 |
| `float16`  | 275.9 |

### Before this PR:

| `dtype` | Backward Bandwidth (GB/s) |
| ------- | ------------------------- |
| `float32`  | 195.5 (69%) |
| `bfloat16` | 132.4 (48%) |
| `float16`  | 132.2 (48%) |

### After this PR:

| `dtype` | Backward Bandwidth (GB/s) | Improvements |
| ------- | ------------------------- | ------------ |
| `float32`  | 241.9 (85%) | +24% |
| `bfloat16` | 153.3 (56%) | +16% |
| `float16`  | 153.3 (56%) | +16% |

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -132,12 +122,10 @@ def test_grouped_rmsnorm_equivalence_randomized(
 
     y_impl = impl(x_impl, m_splits)
     y_ref = ref_impl(x_ref, m_splits)
-    torch.testing.assert_close(y_impl, y_ref, **config.tolerance)
+    torch.testing.assert_close(y_impl, y_ref)
 
     grad_output = torch.randn_like(y_impl)
     y_impl.backward(grad_output)
     y_ref.backward(grad_output)
-    torch.testing.assert_close(x_impl.grad, x_ref.grad, **config.tolerance)
-    torch.testing.assert_close(
-        impl.weight.grad, ref_impl.weight.grad, **config.tolerance
-    )
+    torch.testing.assert_close(x_impl.grad, x_ref.grad)
+    torch.testing.assert_close(impl.weight.grad, ref_impl.weight.grad)
comment.body: Relying on default tolerance of PyTorch is okay. Won't fix.

comment.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,337 @@
+from typing import List
+
+import torch
+import triton
+import triton.language as tl
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def _load_row(base, cols, stride, mask):
+    return tl.load(base + cols * stride, mask, other=0.0).to(tl.float32)
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def _grouped_rmsnorm_fwd_kernel(
+    X,  # in [B, H]
+    Y,  # out [B, H]
+    Gamma,  # in [G, H]
+    Rstd,  # out [B]
+    SegIndptr,  # in [G + 1], starting at 0, end at B
+    stride_in_b: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_in_h: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_out_b: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_out_h: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_gamma_g: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_gamma_h: tl.constexpr,
+    HIDDEN_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
+    EPS: tl.constexpr,
+    BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
+):
+    group_id = tl.program_id(0)
+    row_in_group = tl.program_id(1)
+    row_start = tl.load(SegIndptr + group_id)
+    row_end = tl.load(SegIndptr + group_id + 1)
+
+    row_id = row_start + row_in_group
+    if row_id >= row_end:
+        return
+
+    tl.assume(BLOCK_SIZE >= HIDDEN_SIZE)
+
+    cols = tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)
+    mask = cols < HIDDEN_SIZE
+
+    x_base = X + row_id * stride_in_b
+    y_base = Y + row_id * stride_out_b
+    gamma_base = Gamma + group_id * stride_gamma_g
+
+    x = _load_row(x_base, cols, stride_in_h, mask)
+    gamma = _load_row(gamma_base, cols, stride_gamma_h, mask)
+
+    x_sq = x * x
+    mean_sq = tl.sum(x_sq, axis=0) / HIDDEN_SIZE
+    rstd = 1 / tl.sqrt(mean_sq + EPS)
+    tl.store(Rstd + row_id, rstd)
+
+    out = x * rstd * gamma
+    tl.store(y_base + cols * stride_out_h, out, mask=mask)
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def _grouped_rmsnorm_bwd_dgamma_phase1(
+    dGamma_partial,  # out [B, H]
+    dY,  # in [B, H]
+    X,  # in [B, H]
+    Rstd,  # in [B]
+    stride_dgammap_b: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_dgammap_h: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_dy_b: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_dy_h: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_x_b: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_x_h: tl.constexpr,
+    HIDDEN_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
+    BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
+):
+    tl.assume(BLOCK_SIZE >= HIDDEN_SIZE)
+
+    row_id = tl.program_id(0)
+
+    cols = tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)
+    mask = cols < HIDDEN_SIZE
+
+    out_base = dGamma_partial + row_id * stride_dgammap_b
+    dy_base = dY + row_id * stride_dy_b
+    x_base = X + row_id * stride_x_b
+
+    dy = _load_row(dy_base, cols, stride_dy_h, mask)
+    x = _load_row(x_base, cols, stride_x_h, mask)
+    rstd = tl.load(Rstd + row_id)
+
+    out = dy * x * rstd
+    tl.store(out_base + cols * stride_dgammap_h, out, mask=mask)
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def _grouped_rmsnorm_bwd_dgamma_phase2(
+    dGamma,  # out [G, H]
+    dGamma_partial,  # in [B, H]
+    SegIndptr,  # in [G + 1], starting at 0, end at B
+    stride_dgamma_g: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_dgamma_h: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_dgammap_b: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_dgammap_h: tl.constexpr,
+    BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
+):
+    group_id = tl.program_id(0)
+    col_id = tl.program_id(1)
+
+    row_start = tl.load(SegIndptr + group_id)
+    row_end = tl.load(SegIndptr + group_id + 1)
+
+    blk_start = row_start // BLOCK_SIZE
+    blk_end = tl.cdiv(row_end, BLOCK_SIZE)
+
+    dgamma = 0.0
+    for blk_id in tl.range(blk_start, blk_end):
+        row_base = blk_id * BLOCK_SIZE
+        rows = tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE) + row_base
+        mask = (rows >= row_start) & (rows < row_end)
+
+        partial_ptrs = (
+            dGamma_partial + rows * stride_dgammap_b + col_id * stride_dgammap_h
+        )
+        partial = tl.load(partial_ptrs, mask=mask, other=0.0).to(tl.float32)
+
+        dgamma += tl.sum(partial, axis=0)
+
+    dgamma_ptr = dGamma + group_id * stride_dgamma_g + col_id * stride_dgamma_h
+    tl.store(dgamma_ptr, dgamma)
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def _grouped_rmsnorm_bwd_dx_kernel(
+    dX,  # out [B, H]
+    dY,  # in [B, H]
+    X,  # in [B, H]
+    Gamma,  # in [G, H]
+    Rstd,  # in [B]
+    SegIndptr,  # in [G + 1], starting at 0, end at B
+    stride_dx_b: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_dx_h: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_dy_b: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_dy_h: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_x_b: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_x_h: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_gamma_g: tl.constexpr,
+    stride_gamma_h: tl.constexpr,
+    HIDDEN_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
+    BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
+):
+    tl.assume(BLOCK_SIZE >= HIDDEN_SIZE)
+
+    group_id = tl.program_id(0)
+
+    row_in_group = tl.program_id(1)
+    row_start = tl.load(SegIndptr + group_id)
+    row_end = tl.load(SegIndptr + group_id + 1)
+    row_id = row_start + row_in_group
+    if row_id >= row_end:
+        return
+
+    cols = tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)
+    mask = cols < HIDDEN_SIZE
+    dx_base = dX + row_id * stride_dx_b
+    dy_base = dY + row_id * stride_dy_b
+    x_base = X + row_id * stride_x_b
+    gamma_base = Gamma + group_id * stride_gamma_g
+
+    dy = _load_row(dy_base, cols, stride_dy_h, mask)
+    x = _load_row(x_base, cols, stride_x_h, mask)
+    gamma = _load_row(gamma_base, cols, stride_gamma_h, mask)
+    rstd = tl.load(Rstd + row_id)
+    x_hat = x * rstd
+    dy_hat = dy * gamma
+
+    dx = rstd * (dy_hat - x_hat * tl.sum(dy_hat * x_hat, axis=0) / HIDDEN_SIZE)
+    tl.store(dx_base + cols * stride_dx_h, dx, mask=mask)
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def _calculate_seg_indptr_kernel(
+    SegIndptr,  # out [G + 1]
+    MSplits,  # in [G]
+    N: tl.constexpr,
+    BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
+):
+    tl.assume(BLOCK_SIZE >= N)
+
+    offs = tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)
+    mask = offs < N
+
+    m_splits = tl.load(MSplits + offs, mask=mask, other=0)
+    cumsum = tl.cumsum(m_splits, axis=0)
+
+    tl.store(SegIndptr + 1 + offs, cumsum, mask=mask)
+    tl.store(SegIndptr, 0)
+
+
+def seg_indptr_from_splits(
+    m_splits: torch.Tensor,
+) -> torch.Tensor:
+    g = m_splits.numel()
+    device = m_splits.device
+
+    seg_indptr = torch.empty(g + 1, dtype=torch.int32, device=device)
+
+    BLOCK_SIZE = triton.next_power_of_2(g)
+    _calculate_seg_indptr_kernel[(1,)](
+        seg_indptr,
+        m_splits,
+        g,
+        BLOCK_SIZE,
+    )
+    return seg_indptr
